- GLP-1 Newbies: Tips You Need to Know: Kind: captions Language: en If you're about to start as GLP1, let me stop you right there. You don't need to spend weeks preparing. You don't need to overthink it. Just take the shot. There are some things no one tells you, and you won't find out until you're already in it. You're going to run into surprises. From side effects to foods that suddenly don't sit right to why your goal weight doesn't really matter, these are the top tips I wish I had before I took my first shot. If you're new here, I'm Megan. I've lost 125 lbs using compounded turppetide. I started in August 2023, weighing 243 lbs and I've been maintaining a healthy weight for over a year now and today I'm weighing in right around 118 lbs. I started this YouTube channel less than 2 months ago and I'm so happy that the videos that I've been making are connecting with some of you and helping you along your journey. I would love it if you would take a minute to subscribe to my channel so that you don't miss any new videos. I am taking your suggestions. So, go ahead and leave a comment with any video suggestions and I am making videos as fast as I can so that I can share more about my journey on a GLP1 and what I've learned along the way. When I started my GLP1 journey, I knew one person in real life who was taking a GLP1 and I got some really great tips from this friend two years ago. it was really still on the down low. It wasn't as common as it is today. So, I'm so happy that people starting a GLP1 today have so many resources at their fingertips to learn about what it's like to be on this medicine. And today, I just wanted to share a little bit about what I wish I knew before I started a GLP1 and what would have helped me along on my journey so that I can help some of you who are getting ready to start your journeys today. First of all, if you're about to start a GLP1, the very first step is just go ahead and take that shot right now. Like, pause this video, go get it out of the fridge and take it. If you've been thinking about it and waiting for the perfect time to start, the perfect time to start is now. You don't have to wait until a certain day of the week. You don't have to wait until a certain time of the month. Like, just go and take that shot. The truth is, there is never going to be a perfect time to take the shot. There's always something happening. There's a vacation coming up. you have a holiday weekend, somebody's birthday, you have a night out planned with friends. There's always going to be something. So, the very best time to take that shot is now. That leads me into my second tip, which is yes, you're going to have some side effects, but you'll learn to manage those as they come. You don't need to learn how to manage all the side effects before you take the shot because you might not even have those side effects. There are certain side effects that are more common like constipation or a little bit of nausea, but not everybody gets those things. The friend who I knew in real life who told me about GLP1s, she told me she felt like she had the flu the first week. That did not happen to me, and I'm so thankful for that. I had different side effects. So, there's no real way to know until you just go ahead and take that shot and see what happens to your body. All of our bodies are going to react differently and each of us is going to have different side effects and even the ways to manage those side effects are going to be a little bit different from person to person. We can take the advice from other people, but what works for one person isn't necessarily going to work for you. So, postponing taking your shot until you learn about all these side effects is of no benefit for you because you have to just get in there and deal with it and I promise you'll learn what works for you. Some of the common side effects that I had were a little bit of nausea and eating a few mini saltines solved that problem for me. Just making sure I had a little bit of something in my stomach. That salt crunch, it just helped me out. And if I felt any nausea, I can nibble on those. I also found that if I ate certain foods, I was more prone to nausea. So, if I ate fried foods or super fatty foods or super rich sweet foods, one time I ate a little bit of cheesecake and that did not sit well with me at all and I had nausea. Uh, my friend Gina from Ankor Moments uh has a great term for what happens to you when you eat a food that doesn't agree with you on a GLP1. She calls it a spanking. So, sometimes you can catch the signs of that happening before you get to a full-blown spanking. And I have found that Tums work really well for me. So if I eat something that maybe just doesn't quite agree with my body, I will pop a couple of Tums. And I honestly only have to do that maybe once or twice a month because I eat pretty clean and I choose to eat a mostly whole food diet with a focus on protein and fiber. But if you are going out or to a birthday party or it's a holiday weekend or something like that, you might be eating foods that you're just not used to and you might get signs of a spanking coming on. So having some tools to prevent that from happening is really good. So I like to use Tums. Other people like things like Pepsid or Kepto-Bismol. I have a weird reaction. One time we were at Disneyland for my 40th birthday and we went out for a birthday dinner and ate too much rich food and I had a spanking coming on. So we went into the gift shop and I found some Pepto-Bismol and I took it and it helped thankfully. But the very next morning I woke up and I had a black tongue. I did not know this could happen but I was brushing my teeth and I was spitting black. Of course I freaked out. Thankfully, Google told me that that's a reaction that some people have to the medicine in Pepto-Bismol that you get a black tongue. So, I don't take Pepto-Bismol anymore because I don't want to walk around with a black tongue that takes days to go away. But the Tums works for me. So, if you end up with nausea or signs of a spanking coming on, you'll have to figure out what medicines work for you. But really, the very best thing is to just eat in a way that agrees with your body. Another common side effect is constipation. I honestly had very little constipation. Some people deal with this the entire time they're on a GLP1, but I think sometimes those people have dealt with constipation in their life anyway. I have never dealt with constipation, so I've only had it a few times. I choose to use Oxy Powder. It is a magnesium supplement and it works like a charm for me. The bottle says take four pills and I beg you not to take four pills if you going to take Oxy Powder for your constipation. you will poop your pants. So, go ahead and start with like two pills. If that doesn't work the next day, you take these before bed with a full glass of water. It works overnight. You don't wake up with like a rumbling stomach or anything. You just wake up and you're like, I have to go. If you take four pills, you will have an emergency. So, start with two, see how your body reacts. Go ahead and take three the next day if you still haven't had success. And I have only had to take these a handful of times during my journey because it's just not a problem that I struggle with. Some of the other things that I've dealt with are headaches or fatigue and I've just managed those like I manage any symptoms that I don't know where they come from. If I have a headache, I'll take some Advil. I will rest. I'll put a cold pack on my head. If I have fatigue, I take a nap. I drink some extra caffeine. I just deal with those symptoms as they come up. And honestly, there's no real way to know if those are symptoms of the medication or if they're symptoms of being a sleepd deprived mom who's really busy and just sometimes gets a headache or sometimes is feeling fatigue. I do think some weeks I had fatigue like crazy where I felt like almost like when I was pregnant and in my first trimester and there's just like this bone crushing fatigue where you cannot sleep enough and you just cannot get up like there's no amount of willpower that is going to get me off the couch. I did have that for a couple of weeks and I just dealt with it. Other side effects will just be completely random. I had a few weeks where I had joint pain. I had one rough stretch where I had restless leg symptoms happening. That was the worst because I could not sleep. Again, I just figured out how to deal with each symptom, one symptom at a time, and I didn't have to think about all of that beforehand. I just dealt with it as it came up. I did not have symptoms consistently every single week when I took a shot. I would have a symptom for a week or two, then I would have a different symptom for a week or two, then I'd get something new. So being on a GLP1 is a constant experiment and adventure and it's just something you can't plan for. On to tip number three. So you will learn pretty quickly which foods agree with you and which foods don't. When you're going on a GLP1, you do not need to prepare to change your entire diet and change everything about the way that you eat the day you're starting your shot. Being on a GLP-1 is not like any other weight loss diet that you've tried. I remember I've done things like Whole 30. When I did Whole 30, I spent a good two or three weeks prepping ahead of time because I had to buy all the right ingredients. I had to make sure I had the recipes lined up. I did a meal plan. I had to get rid of stuff in my house so that I wasn't tempted while I was doing Whole 30. But being on a GLP1 is not like that. You can eat how you normally eat. And the shot is going to help you make better decisions. It's going to help you eat less of what you like to eat. It's going to be working in the background of your brain to help you figure out what are the foods that are the best for your body. When you eat those foods, if it starts to make you feel sick, you'll quickly learn that's not a food that I want to be eating. Sometimes people have told me they feel really full with certain foods. Like maybe you're eating broccoli and you just feel like it's kind of stuck in this area and maybe that's not something that you want to eat. instead of broccoli, maybe you switch to something more like green beans if you're looking for an quick, easy vegetable that you can grab from the freezer. I know for me, I had a lot of changes in my taste buds where I was a carb addict before and once I started on a GLP1, I did not like bread items. No bread for sandwiches. I didn't like bread like breakfast breads for pastries. And I loved donuts so much. And for a while, like donuts and pastries and croissants, none of that looked good to me. I would look at it and think, I should like that, but it looked kind of gross to me. So, I just didn't eat it. So, anytime there was a bread involved, I would take the bread off of a sandwich, have a burger without the bun. Not because I was trying to be carb conscious or anything. I just didn't want the bread. Once you're on the medication, your brain is going to help you out and tell you what kinds of things your body wants to be eating. The key is just listening to your body. Be really in tune with what your body is telling you. One great way to do that is by keeping a food journal, tracking what you eat, writing down what you're eating and how you feel afterward. And you'll start to notice some patterns that can help you make decisions when you're planning out what you're going to eat. Moving on to number four. Your body's going to help tell you what it doesn't want to eat, and your brain can tell you what you should be eating. When I started on a GLP1, I had the worst diet. I was a fast food addict. I ate out five to seven times a week, sometimes multiple times a day. And once I started on a GLP1, I knew that eating at home was going to be the very best choice for me to get wholesome, nutritious food that fueled my body in the way that I needed to fuel it so that I could lose weight, gain muscle, and be as strong and healthy as possible. So, I made a choice to focus on eating protein and fiber because when you're on a GLP1, you cannot eat much. In the very beginning, you are eating a small quantity of food. Now, I do want to say that that doesn't last forever. As your body gets used to the medicine and you go on, you can eat more, but the mechanism that works in your brain is still in the background helping you make good decisions. Now, I'm over 2 years into my GLP-1 journey, and I would say I can eat pretty normal meals, but it was not that way in the beginning. In the beginning, I ate very small meals. I didn't have a big appetite. I have a rule for myself that I don't eat when I'm not hungry. So whenever I felt hungry, I made sure that I was focusing on eating protein and fiber. So what that meant is I was eating lots of lean protein like chicken breast, tuna, salmon, or I was eating artificial protein like protein shakes because I needed to get protein in my body. And then I focused on getting fiber. And since I wasn't really into eating bread products, I did a lot of berries, fresh leafy greens, just fresh fruits and vegetables that helped fill me up and taste good and add some excitement to my life. So, I just wasn't eating protein shakes constantly, having some raspberries and Greek yogurt was delicious. Having blueberries and a protein cereal or something like that, just making sure that I was adding fiber into my diet, which again helps with some of those side effects that you might get. and it was a really good way to add variety to my plate when I was still only able to eat a small amount. Tip number five would be to incorporate movement as soon as you are able. I started at 243 lbs. It was really hard for me to even just get through the day. I couldn't think about adding extra exercise. I was doing this not just to lose weight, but to get healthy. So, I knew that movement was going to be a part of my journey. So, I did what I could. I started by just walking around my block, which is half a mile. It took me over 20 minutes to do it. And I just started by doing that every day. You'll have to figure out what is a stretch goal for you. And what are you able to do? And I share that because I want you to be inspired and know that even while it seems impossible now, it is possible for all of us to figure out what we can do. And maybe walking isn't in your future right now. Maybe you just can't imagine that. But you can sit in a chair and use soup cans to lift weights. You can get some exercise bands and start stretching exercise bands. There are lots of things and thank goodness we have YouTube, we have ChatGBT to figure out different ways for each of us to incorporate movement into our day in a way that works for us. We incorporate movement not to lose weight. I've made a whole video and why I don't think exercise is a good tool for losing weight. When we're losing weight, being on a GLP1 and moderating the food intake that is going into your mouth is your number one lever that you can pull for affecting your weight. That's what you need to focus on. We focus on movement because we're also focused on health. We're building a stronger body. When you're losing a lot of weight, there is a potential for losing muscle. I am happy to say while I was losing 125 lbs, I was able to gain a lot of muscle and that is due in large part to eating enough protein and lifting weights as soon as I was able. So in addition to walking, I started lifting weights. I started with just three times a week. I was never somebody who went to the gym or knew anything about exercise. I started using my Tommo home gym because I wanted to be able to work out at home and do it in my pajamas. I started with just three days a week and 20 minute workouts. So once I had lost about 100 pounds, I really amped up my fitness and focused on strength training where I was strength training four to six days a week. And you don't have to get that crazy, but for me that was the key to really transforming my body into the look that I wanted it to have, which was a fit, muscular, lean look. Tip number six is to take pictures. If you're like me, you started a life-changing plan many, many times before, but on a GLP1, this is hopefully going to work for you. This will be the time that it works and that it sticks. So, go ahead, take a before picture. It's going to feel awkward. And take a full light picture. If you have a full- length mirror, take it in a full- length mirror or set your phone up somewhere and flip the camera around and just take yourself a picture. For me, when I was losing weight, it was so hard to see how far I'd come on a day-to-day basis. I would look in the mirror and I would feel exactly the same. And that can be kind of defeating when you're paying a lot of money for these medications, you're putting in all this work, you're eating the right foods, and you're doing the movement that you want to do so that you're healthy, and you're not really seeing the progress. For me, the way that I saw the progress was by looking at pictures side by side. When I could look at my sideby-side pictures, I would say, "Oh my gosh, like look at the difference." I could see the difference in my stomach. I could see the difference in my thighs. It just helped bring the full picture to life that I was making progress every single day. Even though I couldn't see it, once I did that side by side, it was so much motivation to keep going. And that also leads to tip number seven that you need to have some nonscale metrics for your success. So things like taking measurements. Go ahead and take those measurements. I remember I took measurements and I had a 42 in waist. It started shrinking immediately. My waist was shrinking. My arms started shrinking immediately. They had gotten so big. That was just one of the first things to go. So seeing the change in the measurements is huge. Again, it's just that motivation that you need to see that even if the scale isn't going down as much as you think it should, that you are making progress along the way. When I look back and see that I started at that 40in waist and now I have a 26 in waist, that just shows me how far I've come. Other things you can do are things like getting your blood work done. Get your blood work and see where you are as your starting point. Don't be afraid of getting blood work. I know I avoided the doctor and avoided getting blood work for so many years, but have a starting point. You can repeat your blood work in six months and it will be an amazing change. Another thing that I did was I got DEXA scan. I was able to get a scan of my body and see where my muscle was, where my fat was, what my visceral fat looks like around my stomach and then do that along the way while I was making progress and see the huge changes I was making in my body. And those are the kinds of changes that are more important than weight loss. Yeah, weight loss is going to make us look better and feel better, but reducing our visceral fat or building our muscle, those are the things that are going to help us live longer and be healthier while we're living longer. And that really was the goal for me. So, I love that I had all of that data to go into the full picture of how much progress I was making along the way. And that leads into tip number eight, that you should have a doctor or a medical professional that you can talk to on your GLP1 journey about some of these things that we're talking about today. Now, I know a lot of us get compounded medications from teleaalth companies and I have used several of them and I will say that not all teleaalth companies are created equal. I know the company that I currently get my medication from, I have never talked to a doctor. They prescribe it to me. I only had to fill out a form and they send it to me. If I had a question, I'm sure they would answer it, but I have no relationship with any medical professional. Now, I am comfortable with that because I've been on this medication for 2 years. When I started out, I worked with a local nurse practitioner at a med spot. They prescribed it for me. I had to do a phone consultation every four weeks. They would ask me about my side effects, how's it going? They looked at my blood work, things like that. And I think that that is a really good thing to have somebody who you can talk to about questions you have that are very specific to your body and your unique situation. I also have a primary care doctor and I made sure to tell them upfront, hey, I'm taking a GLP1. I got it from this med spa and I want you to do my blood work so that I can track how I'm doing along the way. They were completely happy to do that. They did not prescribe compounded medications and my insurance doesn't cover the name brand. So, I couldn't get it through them. So, they were happy to follow me and track my progress even though I wasn't getting the medication from them. Eventually, they started offering compounded medications and I did get it through them. And then I was able to go in for appointments and get seen by a nurse. But having an actual medical professional available to you is so important. And my last tip that I wish I knew is that goal weights are really kind of useless. And I don't want you to not take your shot until you figure out your goal weight because it really is irrelevant. Instead of having a goal weight, I have learned that it is so much better to have a goal life. I like to think about all the things that are going into the life that I want to have. So, at the very beginning, I was 243 lbs. I was exhausted. Every bone in my body achd and hurt. I took a 2-hour nap every day and I was so tired from carrying around all this extra weight. The life that I wanted to live was so completely different than the life that I had and it seemed so far away. So when I thought about what success looked like and I thought, okay, success would be being around 160 lbs for me. That was the comfortable weight that I could maintain for most of my adult life even after having children. So I thought, well, that will be great. Once I got to that point and I saw what was possible with a GLP1, I knew that I could go ahead and do more. I didn't feel comfortable in my skin yet. I drink my water. I eat my protein. I get in fiber. I manage my symptoms and my side effects of being on this medication. I just continue to do those things every single day. And that was my life. That was my goal life. I wanted to be healthy and strong and fit. This isn't like a diet like when I did Whole 30 and I just had to make it through those 30 days and then I could go back to eating the way I used to. This is a lifestyle. It truly is the way I want to live my life for the rest of my life. So what my body does with that is kind of not up to me. I just go on about my business eating the way I do and living the way I live and my body is going to go where it wants to. So for me, while I started out with that goal weight of 160, then I got to like 145 lbs, which is a healthy weight for my height. I'm 5'4 and I started decreasing my dose because I thought I'm at a healthy weight. I was super comfortable in my skin at that point. So, I started amping up the exercise and instead of maintaining my weight, my body just kept losing weight. I kept going down in weight and it doesn't keep on going forever. You're not going to waste away. Most people I've talked to who have been on these medications for a long time and who have reached a maintenance weight. The body just kind of reaches this equilibrium where it works out with what you're eating and how you're moving results in a weight that is right for your body. So for me that's right around 118 lbs. Sometimes it goes up a little. I've gone uh up as high as like 126 lbs. Then I'll go down. I've been as low as like 112 lb. But for the most part, my body is really maintaining at 118 lbs easily with no extra effort. And again, I'm not eating super small amounts anymore. I've been on this medication for over 2 years now, so I'm eating pretty normally. I eat anywhere from 1300 to 21,00 calories in a day depending on how hungry I am or what kind of activity I have going on. And I think that's pretty normal. - When to Increase or Decrease Your GLP-1 Dose (Doctor Explains): Kind: captions Language: en One of the most common questions I get from my patients when I start them on a GLP-1 medicine is, "How should I know when to increase my dose of my dose?" Hey, I'm Dr. Spencer Nadulski, a triple board certified physician in family medicine. I specialize then in obesity medicine and lipidology. I've overseen thousands of patients and overseen tens of thousands of patients if you include all the doctors that I've overseen over the years. CEO and founder of Vineyard, where we actually give comprehensive metabolic and obesity care online. the trials, whether it's the step trials or the surmount trials, that's for Wiggoi or Zepbound, they generally just crank them up at some sort of protocol regardless of the rate of weight loss and how they tolerate it. Some of the later trials do try to accommodate for intolerances to the medicine and they adjust it based on that. But none of them adjust it according to weight loss. So, for example, if somebody's losing weight rapidly faster than let's say 2% of their body weight. So, let's say if you're 200 lb, that would be 4 lbs per week. If somebody's losing that quickly, they're not adjusting their dose down in these trials. However, in practice, we generally try to keep them at the same dose if they are losing that quickly. I want to tell you exactly how I do it in my practice. I've overseen thousands of patients myself and overseen tens of thousands of patients if you include all the doctors I've overseen over the years. This is the protocol that we do. Now, I do want to preface this by saying there has not been a randomized trial looking at how we do it in clinical practice versus the trials that have just increased their dose no matter what. I can't tell you who's going to be doing better in the long run if you just crank people up to the highest dose as quickly as possible versus going slower. But given what we know about other diets and different types of weight loss trials, I think this guidance will give you the best shot. So here's what we do. Number one, side effects always come first. If someone's feeling really nauseous from this medicine, it would be really silly to increase their dose. Now, of course, insurance sometimes wants you to go up no matter what cuz that's how the trials were done. That's what's on the label of the prescription. However, if somebody's throwing up at, let's say, tzepide or zeppbound 2.5 millig and you go up to 5 millig, they're probably going to get sicker. So, number one is always side effects. Look at side effects. I generally do a scale of 0 to five, zero being no side effects at all, five being severe, uh nausea, throwing up, constipation, vomiting, all those different types of things. So, if somebody is a two or less on a scale of five, again, this is just my scale that I use, it's probably okay in the side effect profile. Again, it's subjective, so you can always talk with the patient to know. So, side effects are number one. If somebody's having a lot of side effects, three, four, five out of five, have that discussion. Probably don't want to go up in the dose. Again, insurance can push back, and that's when you have to write a letter, yell at the insurance, and say, "No, no, no. This person's not tolerating the medicine. Please don't make them go up in the dose." Once you get past side effects, what I look at is rate of weight loss. In general, for these medicines and in weight loss in general, we look to see about a 0.5 or 0.5% to 1% of total body weight loss per week. So, for example, let's say you're 200 lb. 1% of body weight per week is 2 lb per week. 0.5% is 1 lb per week. If you're in that range, you're going at a good rate. The reason we picked this rate of weight loss is because in general when you go faster than 1% total body weight loss per week, you do risk increasing formation of gallstones in your gallbladder. These medicines do come at a risk of increasing gallstones and having gallbladder attacks. So ideally to prevent this we would go a little bit slower at least controlled uh weight loss to prevent that. The other thing is that faster weight loss may actually increase your risk of muscle loss. No matter what the method, whether it's these medicines or if it's diet alone. So, I like to shoot for 0.5% to 1% total body weight loss per week. If you're going over that, it might be water weight in the beginning. So, in the first few weeks, you may actually increase and go to that 1.5, sometimes even 2% of total body weight loss right in the beginning. And then things slow down and even out over time. But in general, after that first month, it should be in that 0.5 to 1% total body weight loss per week. If somebody's going faster than that, don't want to go up in their dose. And in sometimes, you even have to decrease the dose if they just keep losing weight. The other thing is, how far should somebody lose weight? If somebody's already a relatively leaner compared to someone who has a lot of weight to lose, let's say the person only has a 27 BMI, body mass index, and has like 30 lbs to lose to put them in a a range where all their weight related issues go away. How low should they go? In general, I don't like to go much below 21 BMI. I've gone as low as 20 BMI. Some of my patients end up having a 19 BMI and they kind of hover between that 19 to 20 BMI. I absolutely do not go below that though and a lot of times I actually have to decrease the dose. If somebody's going lower than 0.5% of the total body weight loss per week and they don't have side effects, it's time to probably move up in the dose. The other thing to consider, if somebody's in that range of that 0.5 to 1% of their total body weight loss per week, but they're really hungry and they're probably not going to stick to that rate for the next month, we generally go up. This way, we can prevent weight plateaus before they happen. So, we always look at appetite when we're talking to our patients as well. So, they might not have side effects. They might be losing at a good rate, but they're pretty hungry. It's likely you're going to need to go up in the dose. The next thing I look at is actually preference. So, let's say that they don't have any side effects. Let's say that they're losing weight at a really good rate. And you could go either way. You could go up in the dose. You might want to stick to the dose. And the person says, "I want to stick to the same dose and everything else is good." then we're going to stick to the dose because you might as well talk to the patient and see what they prefer. If they want to go up, you're probably going to be able to go up. If they're losing weight too quickly, you say, "Hey, listen. I think we should just stick to this. You're losing weight pretty quickly." And if they want to go up, you'd be like, "M, let's talk about this a little bit more." If they're having a lot of side effects and they want to go up, you'd say, "Well, why do you want to go up? You're having a lot of side effects. You're going to get more." But in general, if all things being equal, you go with a preference of what the person wants to do. And lastly, I actually take into account cost. Let's say somebody is self-pay and doesn't necessarily have a lot of money to pay for these medicines. They're going to Lily Direct. They're paying $4.99 a month for their dose of medicine. All things being equal, I'm a little bit more aggressive about these patients because we might as well try to get a little bit more weight loss bang for their buck. There you have it. That's how I decide whether or not to go up or down or stay the same on my patients doses.

So, when it comes to protein, I want to do a quick little nutrition lesson, if you will. There are three main macronutrients. There are carbohydrates, there are fats and there is protein. Carbohydrates is like a quick source of energy for our body and so it breaks it down into glucose or sugar and it is immediately available for our body to use. That's why some people like to eat more carbohydrates before they work out or before they go on a long run because it's easily available fuel source. Fat is a little bit different. We don't necessarily have to get into that right now. But protein, protein is made up of amino acids. And amino acids are like little Legos. They literally build everything in our body. Protein, we need protein to survive. If we don't get enough protein, our body says, "Hey, I'm going to start using the protein that you already have on your body as fuel because I need to get it from somewhere." If you're not taking it in from your nutrition, then we're going to take it from your glutes or from your biceps. Not exactly in that exact way, but it's going to take it from your existing muscle stores. If you're not eating enough calories overall, your body, again, you're not intaking the fuel. It has to get it from somewhere else. So, yes, it's going to pull from some of the fat stores, but it's also going to pull from some of those protein stores. So, it's tricky because on a GLP1, it's hard to eat. I mean, that's part of what what it does, right? It lessens your appetite. And we obviously want to be getting a fairly robust amount of micronutrients and macronutrients in our diet over the days and over the weeks. So my biggest piece of advice that I wish I could have given myself 12 months ago, I did prioritize protein, but make protein non-negotiable. So no matter what I did, I would have to hit at least 100 gram of protein every single day. That sounds like a lot and it feels like a lot on the days when you don't have much of an appetite, but it is doable. Someone has high blood pressure or diabetes or depression, do they look in the mirror and do they say, "You're such a disappointment. I can't believe you failed again. Why can't you get it together?" There's so many important reasons to stay hydrated, and one of them is to keep your GI tract hydrated because, as you may know, when you're on a GLP-1, your digestion moves more slowly, and one of the simplest ways to avoid side effects and to keep your gut healthy is to stay hydrated. The other way is to eat enough fiber. Even if you're eating really healthy, you can have all kinds of GI discomfort from GLP-1s because your digestion is moving slower, and you can have constipation or diarrhea because they're just very common side effects with these medications. The recommended amount of fiber per day for women is 25 to 35 g, and I know there are so many people out there it's really popular to take like fiber gummies and fiber supplements to hit that 25 to 35 g of fiber per day, and I don't want to rain on anyone's parade cuz we all have to do what works for us, but I am of the opinion that hitting 25 to 35 g of fiber a day is not that hard most of the time. If you're eating a lot of whole foods, it's honestly not that much fiber. It's not even that much food. I go into this in depth in an episode that I'll link to below. There's a special chapter all about fiber. It's like a deep dive. So, if you want to know more about it, I'll put the title of the episode up here cuz I don't remember it off the top of my head. But, yeah, there's like a whole lesson about fiber. Most days I get all of my fiber from foods. And one of the ways I do that is to make sure to eat certain foods really often, and one of them is number seven on this list, chia seeds. One tablespoon of chia seeds has 3 g of fiber in it. You put a couple tablespoons of these in chia seed pudding and you're hitting 6 g of fiber, which is, you know, almost a quarter of your daily fiber. Mix them with yogurt or milk for a pudding with fruit. Yeah, they're really good for you, and I have relied on them. I don't think I eat them every day, but I rely on them at least three or four times a week. - Mounjaro: Who Loses the MOST Weight?: Kind: captions Language: en-CA Who loses the most weight on Mounjaro? An analysis of the four SURPASS trials that looked at Mounjaro’s effects on weight loss has yielded some interesting results, and helps us understand who is likely to benefit the most from Mounjaro and who may not benefit as much. Mounjaro, also known as the drug tirzepatide, operates in a way similar to semaglutide, which is sold under the two, better-known brand names, Ozempic and Wegovy. Both tirzepatide and semaglutide activate GLP-1 receptors, but tirzepatide has an additional impact on another receptor known as GIP. Just like semaglutide, tirzepatide comes in two brand names: Mounjaro, approved for diabetes treatment, and Zepbound, which is approved for weight loss. Mounjaro and Zepbound are essentially the same. They even use the exact same doses. The only difference between them is the FDA approved label, and likely insurance coverage. Likewise, Ozempic and Wegovy are quite similar, although Wegovy uses a slightly higher maximum dose compared to Ozempic. Recent studies indicate that Mounjaro or Zepbound may be slightly more effective in terms of blood sugar control and weight loss, with fewer side effects when compared to Ozempic and Wegovy. However, it’s worth noting that these impressive results from studies are often presented under ideal conditions, and weight loss medications tend to showcase their best outcomes. So, just because these studies achieved up to a 15% or 20% weight loss with Mounjaro doesn't guarantee the same results for everyone. However, thanks to a recent analysis, we now have a somewhat better idea of who is more likely to experience significant weight loss with Mounjaro. As a quick side note, when the study mentions that Mounjaro is more effective or leads to greater weight loss in a particular group, it means that Mounjaro has a higher likelihood of causing at least a 15% weight loss in that specific group of people. Now, let's consider the first set of factors, which are related to demographics. It was observed that women tend to lose more weight than men when using Mounjaro. This finding isn't entirely surprising because previous studies on various weight loss medications and surgeries have also shown similar trends. While we can't pinpoint the exact reason why women tend to lose more weight, it's believed to be connected to differences in how fat is distributed in their bodies. Additionally, women often have more fat stores to begin with compared to men, so they have more to lose. So, while not groundbreaking news, it's still an interesting insight. The medication also appeared to be more effective in younger patients. But it’s important to note that the average age of patients enrolled in these studies were mostly individuals in their 50s and 60s. So when we say "younger," we're typically talking about individuals in their 40s and 50s, not people in their 20s or 30s. On the other hand, older patients, likely in their 70s, didn't experience as significant weight loss. This also aligns with general trends in weight loss, where younger individuals tend to respond better to various weight loss methods, like surgery, while older individuals tend to see more success with dietary and lifestyle changes. Mounjaro also seemed to work better for individuals of White or Asian races, while African Americans and American Indians/Alaska Natives had lower odds of achieving substantial However, it's important to understand that the majority of participants in these studies were White, with less than 25% being ethnically non-white. Therefore, the study primarily focuses on the outcomes for White patients, and this disparity may not accurately reflect how Mounjaro performs in the broader population. Another factor they considered is the other medications that patients may be using. Mounjaro was primarily tested in individuals with diabetes, and the results indicated that patients who took the anti-diabetic drug metformin alongside Mounjaro experienced more significant weight loss. This finding isn't too surprising, as metformin is known to have some, although relatively mild, effects on weight loss on its own. Finally, they explored a set of factors related to a patient's health history. It turns out that individuals who were in better health before starting the treatment tended to lose more weight. Certain health measurements were linked to greater weight loss. For diabetic patients, starting with lower blood sugar levels and HbA1c values resulted in more substantial weight loss. Additionally, those with better lipid profiles, including lower LDL and total cholesterol levels and higher HDL levels, tended to achieve better results with Mounjaro. On the flip side, patients who didn't experience as much weight loss with Mounjaro typically had poorly controlled diabetes at the outset, and poorer lipid profiles, like higher cholesterol. This suggests that individuals with diabetes or more severe metabolic issues often face greater challenges when trying to lose weight, primarily due to metabolic problems. Factors such as reduced fat metabolism, inflammation, and a diminished response to satiety hormones can all contribute to a slower rate of weight loss for these individuals compared to those with better metabolic health. An interesting side note is that a person's initial body weight didn't seem to influence the impact of Mounjaro. In other words, whether someone was heavier or lighter before starting Mounjaro didn't significantly affect the percentage of body weight they ultimately lost. In summary, several factors appear to influence how much weight a patient can expect to lose Younger individuals, especially women, and those of White or Asian backgrounds tend to achieve greater weight loss results. Diabetic patients who combine Mounjaro with metformin tend to do better. Additionally, individuals with lower blood sugar and better lipid levels before starting Mounjaro tend to experience more significant So, if your blood sugar or lipid levels are currently not well controlled, taking steps to manage them through proper diet and exercise before starting Mounjaro may enhance its weight loss effects. If you're already using another anti-diabetic medication, working with your healthcare provider to switch to metformin could also potentially boost the weight loss benefits of Mounjaro. Obviously some of these factors, like one's race, are beyond a patient's control. Nevertheless, this information can still offer clinicians and patients insights into what to anticipate when using Mounjaro and how to optimize its usage. It's important that we recognize the limitations of these studies. One significant issue is their relatively short duration, lasting only about 40 or 42 weeks, which is just under a year. It's challenging to predict whether these differences in weight loss will remain as pronounced after a full year or two of taking the medication. It wouldn't be surprising if these differences in weight loss became less prominent or even disappeared by the end of the second year. Additionally, as mentioned before, the majority of participants in the studies were White, which doesn't provide the greatest representation of the general population. To better understand how an individual's race might affect Mounjaro's effects, we would need studies that encompass a more diverse range of participants. What about Ozempic and Wegovy? Do the same factors apply to semaglutide, which is what these medications are based on? Unfortunately, there hasn't been a specific analysis like the one done for Mounjaro on semaglutide, which is currently more popular, especially for weight loss. However, there is one study that examined real-world use of Wegovy and found some similar trends. Firstly, it confirmed that women tend to lose more weight than men, which aligns with what we've discussed before. Secondly, it observed that older age was associated with less weight loss, which isn't surprising either. Thirdly, it revealed that individuals with diabetes tended to lose less weight, mirroring the findings from Mounjaro's analysis. This study also showed that those taking metformin alongside semaglutide tended to lose less weight. However this actually helps to confirm our previous point: that diabetics tended to lose less weight than healthy individuals. This is because individuals taking metformin usually have diabetes, which, due to their health condition, results in less weight loss compared to healthier, non-diabetic individuals not requiring metformin. Meanwhile, Mounjaro’s analysis only examined diabetics. So the metformin likely did help diabetics lose weight on semaglutide, like it did with Mounjaro, but even then, their weight loss was less than that of non-diabetic individuals. Interestingly, those taking a different medication called linaclotide, with semaglutide, showed improved weight loss outcomes. Linaclotide is commonly prescribed for people with Irritable Bowel Syndrome and chronic constipation. It's important to note that comparing these two studies to each other is not perfect. The Mounjaro analysis focuses on tightly controlled research trials, while the semaglutide analysis looks at real-world use. Nevertheless, because they work in a similar way and share some similarities, it's reasonable to assume that some of the findings for Mounjaro may also hold true for Ozempic and Wegovy. At the end of the day, achieving more than a 15% weight loss, as seen in some patients using Mounjaro or Wegovy, is typically only seen in ideal scenarios with specific individuals. In reality, most people like you and me using these medications in the real world probably won’t be losing nearly as much. Hi, I’m Dr. Brian Yeung. Do you use Mounjaro? And what are your experiences? Let me know in the comments below. Subscribe and hit the notification bell to stay up to date, and if you found it informative, leave me a like, and share this video with someone you know can use the info.

Oral simaglletide, the pill version of a/mpic, has been around for a while under the name ribbelis. and we've been primarily using it to treat type 2 diabetes, but overall it was kind of disappointing. Like, yeah, it could help with your blood sugars and yeah, it might help a little bit with weight, but it wasn't anywhere near what we saw with WGOI and certainly not what we're seeing with Zepbound. And that's because the max dose is 14 milligs per day, which on a good day works out to be about 1 mg of WGOI once weekly. And the results, well, they really weren't all that game-changing. Now, you might be thinking, well, how can that possibly be? A 14 mig pill versus a 1 mgram injection. How is the pill not mindblowingly more effective? Well, you see, there's a major catch. Simaglletide, the actual molecule itself, is really, really big. And even with some fancy pharmaceutical wizardry, only a small little slice of that 14 milligs actually makes it into your body when you take the ribbus pill. And even then, it's not a stable process for that molecule to get absorbed from your GI tract. Food, drinks, all of those sorts of things have to be taken into account and timed properly or you're not going to absorb any of the medication. And that's why the rules with it are so strict. You got to take it first thing in the morning, completely fasted, 30 minutes before any food, medication, or what have you, and with no more than one cup of water. But now the Oasis 4 trial came out and they tested a 25 mgram dose of this amaglatide tablet. And the results, well, they're pretty darn eye opening. People who followed the routine of taking the pill every single day without missing a dose lost about 16.6% of their baseline weight over the trial period, compared to the placebo group who only lost 2.7% of their baseline weight. Now, to make that more real for you, if you start at say 230 lb, that's about 38 lbs gone after you get to the end of the trial by taking the 25 mg tablet compared to only losing about 6 lb if you were in the placebo group. And when we break things down by weight loss category, such as number of people that lost 5% from baseline, 10% from baseline, and so on, we get some pretty wild results. As you can see here, over 70% of people lost 10% of their baseline weight. More than half the people in the trial lost greater than 15%. And about a third of people actually went on to lose 20% of their baseline weight. So that's not meh ribbelis. That's like wo wovi. But again, there's a catch because there's always a catch. If you remember, I talked about those strict rules. Well, to get results like this, you got to take that tablet every single morning. Again, 30 minutes before any food, any drink, any other medications and with no more than one cup of water. If you mess that step up, well, barely any of the drug actually gets absorbed. That's kind of like trying to water your plants, but not actually turning on the hose. Now, of course, there was side effects, and I would be remiss if I didn't mention them, but they were the pretty standard ones that we always see. And good news, nobody died. But the usual suspects were nausea, constipation, vomiting, and diarrhea, as well as some heartburn, and a whole lot of burpin that people got. In fact, about 74% of the participants had some kind of GI side effect by taking the tablet. And anecdotally, in practice, I've also found that ribbelis really doesn't play nice for some people. Some of my patients have told me that just sitting through that 30 minutes with no food and just letting the drug sit and do its thing, well, that can lead to some major GI upset for them. Now, let's change gears a little bit here and let's ask the question, who is this drug actually going to be for? Well, first and foremost, if needles are your sworn enemy and the thought of doing a once weekly WGO injection makes you want to faint, well, this could be your game changer in the weight management space. Secondly, if you're somebody that thrives on routine, this whole built-in taking a tablet every single day could serve as a perfect reminder that, hey, I'm doing something about my health. I'm trying to improve who I am. And this tablet is a daily reminder of that and is my first step to get the day going. It's like brushing your teeth. It keeps you checked in, gives you a daily rhythm, and keeps you going on the journey that you want to go. But on the flip side of things, if you're someone that struggles to take a multivitamin every day, well, there might be a little bit of a challenge with this strict rule of taking a tablet once a day. Now, thirdly, some people are suggesting that the tablet would be great for maintenance. - Ozempic is a game-changer. Here’s how it works.: Kind: captions Language: en This is a Ozempic. It's a drug approved by the FDA to manage type 2 diabetes. Maybe you've heard the jingle. [singing] “Oh, oh, oh Ozempic.” Or maybe you've seen Ozempic in the news recently for another reason. [overlapping] “Weight loss...” “... weight loss...” “Easy weight loss.” “Miracle Diet Drug.” “Miracle weight loss drug.” And it's true. Ozempic was designed to help people manage their blood sugar... but it's also proven to help people lose weight. My doctor was like “This is mainly for blood sugar management.” “But as a side effects like you will lose weight.” When I started to notice that, like in the first month or so had dropped 10 pounds, like nothing. I was like, “There's something to this.” And once you learn why it works you'll understand how this drug could completely change the way we approach weight loss. According to the latest data from the National Institutes of Health around 42% of adults in the US are currently categorized as obese. The CDC's list of health risks associated with obesity include heart disease, stroke, and type 2 diabetes... diseases that rank among the leading causes of premature death worldwide. Not everyone living with type 2 diabetes is considered obese but the two are closely linked. In fact, one of the leading recommendations for managing type 2 diabetes is weight loss. Even like a small amount of weight reduction for people with diabetes can help blood sugar management. I'm Mila Clarke. I'm 33 years old and I have a type of diabetes called latent autoimmune diabetes in adults. I reached out to Mila because she runs a YouTube channel... Hangry Woman, where she makes videos about living a healthy life with diabetes. The thing that I wish that people understood more is how difficult it is to live with both diabetes and obesity. She's also documented her experience with Ozempic. It has always been really hard for me to lose weight despite diet and exercise and paying attention to lifestyle habits. For a long time, doctors have had mixed views on how to make weight loss more accessible... to help people lower their risk for illnesses like type 2 diabetes. One option is weight loss medication. Historically, weight loss drugs have been associated with dangerous side effects and have been pulled from the market. Metabolic bariatric surgery is extremely effective for weight loss but it requires dramatic lifestyle changes that can be challenging to maintain. But with new diabetes drugs like Ozempic... scientists have a safe and effective tool for weight loss... and it's based on something that's inside our bodies and already: Hormones. Hormones are the body's messengers running from one place to another prompting certain cells into action. When we eat our bodies produce a bunch of hormones to aid in the process of digestion. One of them is GLP-1: the hormone that tells us when we're full. This is a sequence of amino acids that make it up. And this is Semaglutide, the generic name for Ozempic. It's made to mimic GLP-1. Here's why. When food enters your stomach and begins to work its way through the digestive system... your body releases GLP-1 in the intestine. There are receptors for GLP-1 in cells all throughout the body that serve different functions when triggered by the hormone. In the pancreas, GLP-1 receptors promote the production of insulin the hormone that lowers blood sugar levels and suppress the production of glucagon the hormone that raises blood sugar levels. This is important for people with type 2 diabetes whose bodies are insulin resistant and produce very little insulin of their own. Synthetic insulin is a critical treatment for blood sugar management. And semaglutide makes the insulin you do have whether it's produced naturally or synthetically work better in your body. Losing excess fat can also help you become more insulin sensitive. That's where GLP-1’s next receptor comes in. In the stomach, GLP-1 receptors slow gastric emptying... which means food doesn't move through the digestive system as quickly and you stay fuller for longer. It slows the digestion of your food which overall helps with blood sugar spikes and it helps you feel fuller for longer so you don't eat as much. And maybe most importantly, at least in regard to weight loss there are GLP-1 receptors in the brain... particularly in the hypothalamus that suppress hunger cravings. That fuller satiety feeling for longer is really helpful. The digestive system is designed to do all of this naturally but GLP-1 based medications are made to amplify the hormone’s numerous health effects. And it works. A major clinical trial of semaglutide in adults showed an average weight reduction of around 15% when paired with an adjusted diet and exercise routine compared to a placebo group, which demonstrated a little over 2% weight reduction. That level of effectiveness rivals the results of metabolic bariatric surgery. And the fact that I was able to take a once weekly injection and then all of a sudden like the pounds just like dropped off I was like, “This is wild to me.” These drugs aren't without side effects the most common being nausea gastrointestinal effects like diarrhea. Mila initially stopped taking Ozempic because of a side effect that gave her heart palpitations. But she's back on it now. And so far, so good. My blood sugars are great. I'm having an easier time losing weight and I've amped up my exercise and being outdoors and hiking and running and walking. And you still have to do all of the other things that everyone has to do to be able to lose weight. It just makes it a little bit easier. But it's helping me so much with my diabetes management. The number of prescriptions of Semaglutide has risen dramatically since becoming available in late 2017. And in 2021, the FDA approved Semaglutide to be prescribed at a higher dose for weight management specifically branded under the name Wegovy. It was big news and led to this drug getting a lot of attention sort of all at once. The messaging started to center more around weight loss than it did diabetes which is unfortunate for people with diabetes and people living with obesity. There are these two groups of people who really need this drug for health purposes but it's being trivialized and kind of looked at as like “Oh, this is an easy weight loss solution.” There are solutions in place now to deal with the sudden high demand for these drugs for people who need them. receptors throughout the body and when it binds to these cellular receptors it mimics the effects of a substance that is naturally produced by the body and based on the name of these drugs glucagonlike peptide 1 receptor agonists it's probably not going to shock you that our body naturally produces the hormone glucagon like peptide 1 or just glp1 so just to be clear we have the drug the glp1 receptor Agonist mimicking the naturally occurring gp1 but this naturally occurring hormone gp1 is produced by cells called L cells that are found in the intestine as well as produced by neurons within the nucleus of the tractus solitarius that name isn't the most important part of this video but it's kind of fun to say however what is important is that this nucleus of the tractus solitarius is located within the medulla oblongata of the brain stem which is important because these drugs will affect the brain but the take-home message is glp1 is naturally produced and secreted by cells in your intestin and in the brain stem in response to food or in other words when food enters your gut and this hormone does a few important things first glp1 has an effect on the pancreas which you can see here just behind the stomach and it has an effect on two hormones that the pancreas secretes insulin and glucagon now insulin you have likely heard about an insulin decreases blood sugar or what we refer to as blood glucose levels but again blood glucose levels go down when insulin is released whereas glucagon has an opposing effect to insulin glucagon tells your liver to release stored glucose into the bloodstream during times between meals or when you haven't eaten for an extended period of time so glucagon causes blood glucose levels to go up but go1 stimulates the pancreas to release insulin while inhibiting the release of glucose gon so therefore gp1 has an overall effect of lowering blood glucose levels after a mill glp1 also slows gastric emptying in gut motility so food stays in your stomach longer which helps you feel full longer it also acts on the brain to reduce appetite so now that we know how the naturally produced glp1 works we can understand what the drug the glp1 receptor Agonist is trying to accomplish it's going to mimic the effects of the naturally produced glp1 which as we just learned was decreasing blood glucose levels slowing gastric emptying and even suppressing appetite in the brain but mimic is probably too soft of a word because these drugs actually enhance the effects of the naturally produced glp1 because here's the thing about the natural glp1 that's produced in your body it doesn't last very long in your bloodstream or in other words it has a very short half-life if you haven't heard of halflife before it's just referring to the time it takes for the concentration of a substance or chemical to decrease by half and the halflife of the glp1 produced by your body is about 1 to 2 minutes but one of the design characteristics that the pharmaceutical companies were trying to incorporate into these glp1 receptor Agonist drugs was to increase their Half-Life so that they would last longer in the bloodstream and they did it for example the halflife of semaglutide is about one week so going from 1 to 2 hours to one week is quite the dramatic change and this obviously will cause a more enhanced or longer effect of lowering blood glucose levels slowing gastric Ting and suppressing appetite so you can probably start to see how glp1 receptor Agonist such as semaglutide could be utilized for something like type 2 diabetes and this is originally what semaglutide branded as OIC was developed for because as we know those with type 2 diabetes will have elevated blood glucose levels and this drug was an effective tool to help bring those levels down but they also noticed that people were often losing weight on these drugs and this can be attributed to the Drug's ability to slow gastric emptying making someone feel full longer and the effect on the brain to reduce appetite both of which were causing people to eat less and So eventually something like semaglutide was branded at a higher dose as we GOI and this was eventually approved by the FDA for weight loss and there has also been other newer gp1 receptor Agonist approved for weight loss and there's tons of research in other Pharmaceutical compan companies trying to develop newer better forms of these glp1 receptor Agonist but studies were also showing that some of these glp1 receptor agonists were also reducing the risk of cardiovascular related events like a stroke or heart attack in people that already had a pre-existing cardiovascular condition now the exact reason as to why these medications can potentially reduce cardiovascular risk in some people is still being investigated but some of the going theories are that possibly reducing blood glucose levels was contributing to this or could it be from the weight loss because as people tend to lose high amounts of adapost tissue their cardiovascular risk also goes down or was it a combination plus there's also some evidence to support that these medications reduce inflammation within the cardiovascular system either way there seems to be this additional cardiovascular benefit in people that had pre-existing cardiovascular conditions so what's the catch you've got these medications that can help with diabetes help you lose as much as 15% of your body weight and now there's potentially cardiovascular benefits for some populations of people side effect cost the most common side effects of these medications are GI related side effects the most common of those being nausea but other common GI side effects include vomiting diarrhea or constipation bloating and abdominal pain and many of these can be attributed to the slowing of gastric empting now when people first get on one of these medications they are actually supposed to start at a lower dose and then gradually increase until they get to their maintenance dose and many people will often notice that these side effects can lessen over time While others may not be as lucky but people have also experienced fatigue headaches and some of the more serious side effects now I should say that these more serious side effects are relatively uncommon but still worth mentioning pancreatitis is a potential side effect the mechanism of how this occurs is not totally well understood but it is thought that due to glp1 receptor agonis stimulating the pancreas it could potentially cause an overgrowth of some of the pancreatic cells which could then block the ducts of the pancreas leading to a backup of pancreatic juices resulting in inflammation gallbladder issues are another concern is there may be a link between glp1 drugs and gall stones possibly possibly due to the rapid weight loss and or decreased gallbladder empting now during initial drug development there was a concern for increasing the risk of thyroid cancer however this was shown in rodent studies and this has not yet been shown to be the case in humans the last side effect I'll mention isn't serious in the way of it creating an acute condition like pancreatitis but it is still a serious consideration especially in the older patient and that is that the large amount of weight loss that comes from - The Many Lies About The Ozempic Weight Loss Craze: Kind: captions Language: en - Odds are if you've been on TikTok or Twitter as of late, you've seen this new drug, Ozempic, be mentioned. I've actually seen Elon Musk tweet about it. It's been covered in the New York Times. It's literally everywhere. So it's about time that we talk about the truth when it comes to Ozempic. For those who don't know, Ozempic is a medication that's been FDA approved to be used in patients who have diabetes to help control blood sugar and as an added effect, decrease the likelihood of major cardiovascular events and have increases in weight loss. Because of this added effect of weight loss, many people started using the medication off-label for just weight loss. The pharmaceutical company decided to take the same generic medication found within Ozempic called semaglutide, increase its dosage and repackage it under a different brand name called Wegovy and seek FDA approval as a weight loss medication to be used in conjunction with lifestyle changes for individuals who are either obese or overweight but also, who have one weight-related diagnosis like hypertension, high cholesterol, et cetera. And after so many people started getting Ozempic prescribed for this off-label use for weight loss, it exploded in popularity mostly on television and on TikTok, with people claiming that they've lost huge amounts of weight, talking about their successes. And as a result of all this publicity, there was literally a run to doctors to prescribe this medication for them off-label in order to help them with their weight loss which unfortunately, ended up creating a shortage which puts people at risk who desperately need the medication because they have a true diagnosis of diabetes and are not using it for off-label uses. There is a huge rise of misinformation happening at this very moment that we have to fact check. (upbeat music) This claim is patently untrue and anyone saying this has a terrible understanding of how Ozempic, semaglutide, actually works. This medicine actually works as an appetite suppressant that slows or delays gastric emptying, therefore you feel full and you have less of an appetite. So it's more of an appetite modulator, leading you to consume less calories, therefore losing weight, not, "Oh, I can eat 10 pizzas and take this medication and not absorb the calories." You see how there's a very important difference here, because if you eat a lot of food and take this medication that slows your gastric emptying, it's gonna make you feel worse and you're gonna have more side effects, more nausea, more vomiting, more potential diarrhea and constipation. People are actually talking about these side effects who are eating their normal portions or even eating reduced portions. So imagine if you were to overeat, thinking you can while taking this medication, that's a cause for concern. This claim is starting to spiral out of control because it initially started with some simple observations that doctors made of their patients who were losing weight on Ozempic or Wegovy and they were seeing that those patients were losing body fat but they were also losing lean muscle mass. This is not surprising, because the way these medications work is by suppressing appetite, therefore having the patient consume less calories. Anytime you're gonna be consuming less calories, you will both lose muscle mass and body fat. This is obviously not ideal but with this type of medication, that control is limited but we do have control. Any good doctor should not be just prescribing this medication and forgetting about the patient. A, you're supposed to be getting the medication and having the doses slowly increase, based on the effects and side effects that we're seeing. Also, it should come with a plan for nutrition, possibly meeting with a nutritionist who can help guide you as to how much protein you should be taking in despite the fact that you're consuming less calories and a plan for exercise that includes resistance and weight training to help keep on some of that lean muscle mass. This is the one where I'm seeing the most examples of on TikTok. Someone saying, "Oh, I took Ozempic for a wedding and I lost 10 pounds. Look how great I look." This is not what the medicine was intended for. In fact, people who are already of a healthy weight, looking to get skinnier were not the ones that were studied when we were checking the safety and effectiveness of this medication. So maybe, taking it while you're already at a healthy weight can lead to worse side effects. And the worst part of all of it, once you stop taking it, the weight will come right back. This type of yo-yo dieting has been associated with numerous health risks, including returning to your normal weight and then going past it and gaining even more weight after you stop a medication like this. Anytime you have a rapid type of weight loss, what can happen is you lose the subcutaneous fat that's present in your skin, especially if you're in your thirties or forties, and as a result, you can develop this hollowed-out appearance to your face. Now, if you're taking Ozempic or Wegovy the correct way, this shouldn't be happening. My suspicion is the people who are experiencing this quote-unquote, "Ozempic face," or this hollowed-out look are individuals who are rushing to lose weight, using it in a way that is not being prescribed to them. Because when doctors prescribe these medicines, they do it with a titration mindset, meaning they start low and they slowly increase your dose while monitoring how much weight you're losing, how many side effects you're experiencing. Ideally, what doctors are looking for is for a continued one to two pound weight loss per week. Obviously, that varies depending on your own individual situation, but certainly not the 10, 15 pounds in one week that people are claiming they've lost on these medications when they're using them in a non-prescribed, uncontrolled way. I don't think GLP-1 medications should be first-line treatments for those who need to lose weight. Anytime you're prescribing a medication, there needs to be a risk/benefit ratio done for the individual sitting in front of you and ultimately, the individual needs to decide if the medication is right for them. And while these medications have shown promise for individuals to lose 10 to 20% of their weight if they are overweight or obese, it's still important to factor in that there are legitimate risks to taking these medications. There are side effects. People have GI issues, nausea, vomiting diarrhea, constipation, pancreatitis, kidney issues. There is a potential issue of increases in thyroid cancer long-term with these medications. And these are not reasons to not take the medicine. These are reasons to think about this medicine as a tool in our arsenal, to help patients reach their desired weight. And when we talk about their desired weight, that talks about a healthy weight. To me, I feel like this whole conversation has caused us to lose sight of the big picture. With the exception of an individual who's facing harsh socioeconomic standing, someone who has a legitimate medical condition like diabetes or a thyroid condition among the many others that cause increased weight, weight loss or weight gain is under our control. There are things that we can do and I could recommend as a doctor to help someone lose weight when it comes to a nutrition standpoint, a fitness standpoint, sleep, mental health, et cetera. When I think about my patient's health, I don't just think about their weight. Weight is not the sole determinant of one's health. And when it comes to improving someone's lifestyle, we're not only lowering the weight, we're also improving their quality of life, decreasing risks of certain cancers, other diseases and ultimately, extending and prolonging their lives. That's what being a doctor is about, not just focusing on the scale. I actually had a patient whose doctors said all of their symptoms were due to their weight and I actually found, that wasn't the case at all. Click here to check that out and as always, stay happy and healthy.

one of the ways in which they were work is by slowing the emptying of the stomach so one of the side effects not surprisingly can be GI nausea in some cases even vomiting of his diarrhea or bowel issues and in rare cases pancreatitis and inflammation of the pancreas and these drugs do carry a black box warning for a rare type of thyroid cancer so what happens if someone were to take one of these medications like ozempic lose some weight and then discontinue or stop taking the medication obviously it's variable it's not one size fits all and individual results and long-term results will tend to vary but in general we have to remember that the conditions of overweight and obesity are complex chronic disorders that have a multitude of factors leading to them behavior is one of them environment genetics even other medications that may be associated with weight gain are a big part of the picture so if you remove one of those variables in this case a medication that has been helpful in achieving weight loss it is possible if not likely that the person will regain some or all or even more of the weight that they've lost it depends on how much was was lost in the first place it depends on the person and their individual specific factors

- Why Aren't I Losing Weight on Ozempic?: Kind: captions Language: en why aren't I losing weight on OIC quick recap OIC is the medication seatide and stide is marketed at 1 milligram a week for diabetes and is called OIC and it's approved and marketed at 2.4 milligram a week and called W goie for weight loss but regardless of what medication you're on for weight loss it's important to remember that different medications work differently to help you lose weight so one medication may may not be a great fit for you and another may be very beneficial so it's important to have a comprehensive discussion with your physician before choosing the best medication for you but regardless of whichever medication you're on all of them start at a really low dose and go up slowly and it's very common to not lose weight or even see the scale budge at those early low introductory doses really the purposes of those low doses is to help your body get used to the medication and reduce side effects as opposed to seeing weight loss right off the bat the other frustrating thing that can happen is social media is full of claims of Rapid significant weight loss with ozempic when really it's more realistic to see about one or two pounds a week of weight loss when you're first starting the medication it's very common to feel bloated and constipated so you may actually be losing weight and burning fat but the number on the scale may not budge and it can also be frustrating that in the past when you've gone on a really restrictive diet you might have lost weight faster but the key and the goal with this medication is long-term sustainable weight loss maintenance as opposed to a dramatic weight loss over a few months that's really hard to maintain on a super restrictive fat diet it's also important to make sure that you're not burning muscle instead of fat tissue so you want to make sure to keep your protein intake high and to incorporate resistance training into your exercise routine also remember that your weight loss will Plateau eventually you're not going to lose weight Forever on seti because it would be unsafe to do so and again the frustrating piece here is that we can't predict where each person will Plateau to eventually so it's really individualized based on the patient now the most important take home reason why you might not be losing weight on stide is if you haven't reduced your calorie intake in order for this medication to help you lose weight you have to be in a calorie deficit and so if you're eating exactly the same number of calories you ate before you started the medication and you're hoping to lose weight you're simply not going to see that change there's other factors that can also impact or sabotage your weight loss attempt and these can include chronic stress other medications that actually cause weight gain and also things like poor sleep so it's really important to have regular check-ins with your physician and address all aspects of your life and your weight loss Journey hope that helps
