The fact is there are over 80 genes that contribute to the disease of obesity. How we are raised plays a big role as well. Did you know that whether you're breastfed or bottle fed can contribute? That's right. Babies who are breastfed have a lower lifetime risk of obesity than ones who are bottle fed. A bottle fed child is usually fed until the bottle's gone. That don't leave any food on your plate mentality immediately from birth. the breastfed children learn to self satiate, eat until full and then stop. Don't you wish she felt that way now? That last point brings up another issue. Eating when we're not hungry. This can be emotional eating to deal stress, compulsive eating disorders or dysregulation of our satiety centers where a body just does not feel full as quickly as other people. Now, there are many hormonal feedback loops for hunger and feeling full. Ghrellin and Leptin are the main ones. Ghrelin increases our hunger and Leptin reduces it. Obviously, this is a simplification. There's many more hormones at play, but it'll do for this video. Leptin is made in our fat cells. The more fat we have, the more leptin we have. Shouldn't that be a formula for preventing obesity? Sure. But that's not how it works. Our bodies become resistant to leptin because of the chronically high levels, so there's less negative feedback on our hunger drive. Also, when we start to lose weight, our leptin levels plummet. Our body does not like to lose weight. It thinks we're in a famine, right? And it tries to hold on to as much energy as that can. Now, medications such as Saxenda, Wegovy, and Zepbound are called GLP one receptor agonist. This means they activate the glucagon like peptide receptor to induce changes in our body. Now this receptor leads to increased insulin release in response to higher glucose called an increase in effect and why they started as a diabetes medication. You know what else helps treat type two diabetes? Significant weight loss, double benefit! GLP medications are incredibly effective at lowering A1C. I have a patient who took A1C from 9.7 to 5.4 in less than a year. She feels great. The GLP receptors in the gut reduced motility or movement of food through our intestines. This helps us feel full longer and reduce our appetite. It's also why nausea is a major side effect. Your stomach stays distended, giving us that too much feeling if we overdo it or eat too many carbs. Our body wants to shoot them through our system. They also reduce glucagon secretion, which is a hormone that causes the liver to create glucose and raise our serum levels. Additionally, GLP affects the chemistry of our brain and lead to decreased hunger. It has been shown to have anti-inflammatory effects. Cardioprotective effects leading to significantly decreased risk of major adverse cardiac events defined as heart attack, stroke or death in patients with preexisting heart disease. It can reduce nonalcoholic fatty liver disease and steatohepatitis. That's currently the number one cause of liver failure in the United States. It has moved beyond alcohol The most effective GLP medications are injectables, usually once a week. There are oral formulations for the treatment of diabetes, but those have much lower weight loss at the approved doses. Rybelsus, for example, is the same medication as those epic, but in the oral form. Patients on the top dose lost 8 pounds. There are studies being done with massively increased doses of the oral version, which are showing impressive results with minimal side effects. Now, in the coming years, I expect these to be approved for weight loss and give similar results to the current injectables. The SURMOUNT-1 trial showed the weight loss effects with Mounjaro, or Zepbound, Now this is in patients with obesity, but not diabetes. It was a 72 week trial where patients are randomized to receive certain dosing schedules. Amazingly, those on five milligrams just one third the top dose, lost 15% of their body weight on average. The ten milligram group lost 19.5% and 15 milligrams lost 20.9% in 72 weeks. Interestingly, a 50% increase from dosage from the ten to the 15 only led to another 1.4% weight loss. Now, this suggests maybe ten milligrams might be the sweet spot to maximize effects while minimizing side effects because the overall goal is the lowest effective dose of the medication, which is usually the right dose. The placebo arm lost 3%, also showing that placebos work, as does diet exercise counseling, which was part of the study. Now, this means if you weight 300 pounds at the start of therapy, you could on average lose 63 pounds after a year and reduce your weight to 237. This massively reduces your risk of diabetes, heart attack, stroke, arthritis, nonalcoholic liver disease, and many kinds of cancer, and so much more. The good news is there are even better injectables coming. Tirzepatide, again Mounjaro or Zepbound, binds two receptors and offers about a 21% weight loss in just over a year. There are three and four receptor medications coming that are likely to meet or exceed the usual weight loss seen with even Roux En Y gastric bypass. In fact, when I first fit into a pair of size 4 jeans, I didn't believe it, so I had to go buy four other pairs in different brands just to confirm to myself that, yes, your body now fits into a size 4. Do I have products that I use that are helping? Yes. But, it's something that I just wasn't expecting would be as noticeable. And then there's the skin. Oh, the loose skin. I get a lot of questions about this, understandably. You lose 100 lb, your skin is like, "Whoa, we were just holding a lot of stuff, and now where did it go?" So, here's my thoughts on loose skin. Do I have it? Yes. Years ago, I had lost some weight, and I ended up getting an arm tuck and a tummy tuck. So, those areas aren't that bad. There is still some loose skin. My belly is starting to get a little of the creepy skin, especially, but my thighs and my butt, those are the areas where I've always carried the most weight, and those are the areas where I have the most loose skin. I joke that my butt looks like one of those Shar-Pei dogs. Um yeah, there's there's a lot of fat that was lost there. And so, there's a lot of loose skin. Will, over time, it get better? I think so. I think with time, there is a little bit of kind of bounce back. Is it going to be tight and firm? No, it's not going to be. I've thought about the possibility of plastic surgery at some point down the road, maybe, but I know that's a very involved process, so it's it's just a thought. I know it's an option. I have no idea what my decision will be on that. It's also a lot of money. Um but one thing that is in the back of my mind, I'm thinking about the summer. I'm thinking about taking my kids to the beach. We live a 5-minute walk from the beach. I'm going to need to be in a bathing suit. My legs and my glutes are going to be showing. And that makes me a little bit uncomfortable. It makes me nervous. Was I uncomfortable at 237 lb? Yes. So, it's just a different type of discomfort. Is it going to stop me from going to the beach and enjoying the summer with my children? Absolutely not, but it is a thought that crosses my mind. With that being said, would I trade being 237 lb again to not have the loose skin? No, not in a million years. But, I want to be honest about something. For me, and I think maybe for a lot of us, when we think about losing weight, there are so many benefits. Selfishly, and vainly, if that's even a word, one of the benefits is looking better. I like the fact that I look better in clothes now. I like the fact that I feel more beautiful. Do I feel a necessarily more beautiful naked? I don't know. In ways, yes, but I think there's this thought that it's a shame. I feel like it's a shame that I let myself get so big that even though I've lost so much weight, my body was so big and so heavy it will never actually look like like a normal thin person. I don't know if that makes sense, but that's kind of where my brain goes. I could never just go out in a bathing suit, and people think, "Oh, yeah, you've just always been fit." They're going to see me like, "You've lost a lot of weight. You used to be fat. You used to be big." And I think that's something that I have to come to terms with. Another piece of this is that your body changes in ways that you don't necessarily expect. I'm I'm happy with that, but it's just something I wasn't necessarily expecting to happen. Now, here's the part that I almost didn't put in this video, but I think it actually might be the most important piece that I touch on today. Before Zepbound, I had some emotional eating tendencies. I wasn't a binger. That's something that I had worked through early in my 20s, so that wasn't a an issue for me anymore, but there were certainly times where I noticed, if I had a long day, and I was feeling overwhelmed by just the responsibilities of being a parent, of being an adult, I wouldn't want that one more responsibility of having to cook a meal, to worry about making it balanced and healthy, because so much of my responsibilities already involved that, that I would say, "Let's just get takeout." And Friday nights would usually be that night. It'd been a long week. Let's just get takeout. Kids want pizza, fine, we'll get pizza. I I don't care. I just want to relinquish responsibility, and I did that through food. I did that through that meal of "I just don't want to have to worry or take care of anything else right now." My brain's default was just, "Let's not think. Let's eat." Food could definitely be a coping skill for me. It was a comfortable, reliable coping mechanism. It would always be there if I was feeling extra stressed, if I was feeling overwhelmed, if I were was bored. It was always there, my trusty sidekick, if and when I needed it. And here's the thing with emotional eating that a lot of people don't understand. I have done lots of work on this. I was coaching women on emotional eating and body image issues. Even when you know, "I'm emotionally eating right now," your brain will takes over and says, "I know I am, and I don't care. Eff it. I don't care that I'm emotionally eating right now. I'm going to do it anyway." And that's what would happen to me sometimes before Zepbound. And this is what Zepbound did for me. So, the best way I can describe it is before, my emotional hunger was asking for food in response, rather than asking for an emotional response. If I'm emotionally hungry, it makes sense that I would want a a response that is going to help that emotion. If I'm physically hungry, it would make sense that I would go for something to cure the physical hunger. When you are an emotional eater, those wires get crossed. And what Zepbound did for me was it uncrossed the wires. So, I have an emotional issue that I'm dealing with, my brain no longer defaulted to, "Okay, well, why don't you go, you know, order takeout and get pizza?" My brain was like, "Well, hey, how are you going to deal with this emotion? Like, what are you going to do?" So, what I needed to do was I had to sit down, and I wrote it out. I wrote out my list of strategies, and I said, "When I'm bored, I can do this. When I'm feeling tired, I can do this." And the strategies were ones that actually matched the emotional need that I was having at the time. Now, was I aware of this process before Zepbound? Yes, I was. However, what Zepbound does is it gives me the space to actually want to make the choice of meeting my emotion with an emotional strategy. But at first, this was pretty disorienting. Coping It was a coping mechanism that although it did not work, it was comfortable. It was familiar. It was something that I was used to. So, when the medication kicked in and that instant urge to go to food when I was feeling uncomfortable in some way, I was a little confused to be honest with you. So, I had to kind of do some work there and say, "Okay, wait a minute. If my brain is not going to food, what is it going to go to?" And in a way, I kind of had to mourn that mechanism that I was using because it had been something that was serving me at some point in my life. And the GLP-1 gave me the space to be able to build those habits, but it didn't build them for me. That's work that I had to do. I had to go through the strategies and choose them, the ones that would be most helpful to me. And that work is really worth doing. I want to touch on something that I don't think enough people in this space talk about, and that's the fear. I'm 39 years old. I've been on Zepbound for 13 months, and I've lost 100 lb. At night sometimes, I think about "What happens if I have to get off this medication? What happens if something happens and I have to get off?" I will say, ideally, I will be able to get off and maintain. But there's this little voice in my head that says, "Well, what if you get off and you gain it all back?" I think this fear is very real for a lot of us, and I think it's something we need to talk about because I think for so many this is the first time that we've actually lost weight and it's stuck. So, the stakes feel so much higher. It's like we finally got something that works, and your brain wants to say, "Well, what if it doesn't last? What if it slips out of your fingers? You got the taste of this now, but what if it doesn't last forever?" And it's so normal for our brains to do that because every other time in the past, what's happened? We've lost weight, we've gained it back. That's all our brains know. That's the only story that it knows because it happened once, twice, maybe multiple times in your life. It's happened multiple times for me. So, what am I choosing to focus on instead? Because that voice might always be there. Do I want to do whatever I can to turn it down? Yes. And how do I do that? I can focus on something that I can do now. Can I focus on 5 years down the road? No. Do I know what's going to happen? No. But can I focus on building the tools, like building strength, eating to fuel your body, creating healthy habits, thinking of my longevity and thinking long-term, making it work for my life? If I can focus on those habits now, those are the tools that will help me live a healthy, long, vibrant life with or without the medication. So, while the medication is a tool, the life that we're building while we're on it is ours. Nobody can take that away from us. Even if for some reason the medications, poof, they go away, the life that we've created while we're on the meds, that's ours forever and for always. And that's one of the main reasons that I created this guide around my Betty method pillars, the five pillars of building muscle intentionally, eating to fuel, training consistently, thinking long-term, and making it your way, your healthier way. How a new wave of weight loss drugs could transform the diet industry: Kind: captions Language: en Imagine it’s lunchtime and instead&nbsp; of craving this giant bacon double cheeseburger to feel full, all&nbsp;you needed was this little slider. That’s the reality for many patients taking&nbsp; a brand-new kind of obesity treatment. When I took my first dose, the next two days, I felt this satisfied-hunger feeling that&nbsp;I don't think I'd ever felt for years. Wow! Was this how a normal person&nbsp; maybe can feel around food? The secret behind Wegovy and its&nbsp; predecessor Ozempic is an active&nbsp;&nbsp; ingredient called semaglutide, which belongs to&nbsp; a group of drugs called GLP-1 receptor agonists. They work by imitating a naturally occurring gut&nbsp; hormone that helps regulate appetite in the brain. Originally developed to treat diabetes,&nbsp; this class of drugs has taken the&nbsp;pharmaceutical world by storm. They're much more effective&nbsp; than drugs we've had before. They're even more effective than&nbsp; lifestyle modifications on their own. We are getting lots of inquiries,&nbsp; lots of people are desperate to try. Celebrities like Elon Musk and Jeremy Clarkson&nbsp; have helped fuel the excitement, with Hollywood&nbsp;&nbsp; stars and social media influencers touting&nbsp; the effectiveness of these ‘miracle drugs.’ I lost so much weight so fast that I was&nbsp; like, OMG this is magic, this is amazing! I’m going to start Wegovy because I’m a&nbsp;plus-size individual and I don’t want to be plus-size anymore and I want to be healthier. And they’re expensive. Plus, there are concerns about them getting&nbsp; into the wrong hands. But despite these issues,&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP1s could transform the diet market. And experts argue the&nbsp; revolution has only just begun. The latest report by the World Health Organisation showed that obesity has nearly tripled since 1975 and continues to rise. There are more than 1 billion people clinically obese globally, of whom 650 million are adults, 340 million are adolescents and 39 million are children. These medicines are so powerful they are already&nbsp; displacing traditional weight loss programs. In March 2023, shares in WW International,&nbsp; formerly known as Weight Watchers, surged over 70% in one day after the diet company announced a $132&nbsp;million acquisition of Sequence, a subscription&nbsp;&nbsp; service connecting patients with providers&nbsp; which can prescribe these new weight loss drugs. This relatively new drug class was originally&nbsp; developed by Danish pharmaceuticals giant Novo Nordisk to treat diabetes, and it’s been&nbsp; a monumental achievement in diabetes care. But researchers also noticed that patients&nbsp;&nbsp; taking these drugs were losing&nbsp; significant amounts of weight. GLP-1 is a gut hormone that&nbsp;you and myself produce. And it has multiple effects around the body,&nbsp;&nbsp; So it signals to the pancreas to produce&nbsp; insulin when glucose or blood sugars are high. It sends signals to GLP receptors in certain parts&nbsp;of your brain that almost&nbsp;acts as an appetite suppressant. It slows down the emptying of your stomach,&nbsp; so it makes you feel full, so you eat less. Novo Nordisk originally won approval for Ozempic&nbsp;to treat diabetes, then went back to the regulators&nbsp;with more data and got approval for WeGovy,&nbsp;a higher dosage version, to treat obesity. Studies have shown that patients taking&nbsp; a weekly injection of WeGovy achieved&nbsp;&nbsp; 15% weight loss over a 2-year period&nbsp; compared to just 2.6% with a placebo. U.S. pharma giant Eli Lilly is hoping&nbsp; for even better results with its own&nbsp;&nbsp; version in 2023, sold under the brand Mounjaro. WeGovy really sort of changed the paradigm&nbsp; because this is a high dose GLP-1. It is the same molecule that’s&nbsp; in Novo’s best-selling diabetes&nbsp;product called Ozempic but it is at a higher dose. And this drug really is now really&nbsp; changing the paradigm of obesity treatment. You’re getting 15 to 17 approaching 20%&nbsp;weight loss with a weekly injection. Jackie Bannister has struggled with&nbsp; weight for years, and she isn’t alone. The prevalence of obesity&nbsp; in adults from high-income&nbsp;&nbsp; countries is the highest of any income group. After reading reports about celebrities&nbsp; losing weight with these new drugs,&nbsp;&nbsp; she decided it was worth a shot. I do think a lot of people&nbsp; just will say you're not disciplined enough. Which, you know, fair enough. But when you are genuinely feeling hungry,&nbsp; and that's all your brain can focus on,&nbsp;&nbsp; even though you know you've&nbsp; eaten what should be for the day. Like it's hard to ignore. As with any new drug, patients worry about&nbsp; safety and side effects. Dr. Babak Ashrafi is a general practitioner and clinical lead for service&nbsp;expansion at ZAVA, a digital healthcare service. What about side effects, are&nbsp; there any of concern to you? The main ones with this group of drugs are gut&nbsp; related, and a few sent what we call centrally&nbsp;&nbsp; associated side effects. So, the gut related ones are things like&nbsp; nausea, vomiting, occasional abdominal pain, and the centrally acting side effects&nbsp; are things like headache and fatigue. Some of the rarer ones that we ask people to&nbsp; look out for include things like pancreatitis. But, on the whole, these&nbsp; drugs are relatively safe,&nbsp;&nbsp; they don’t tend interact with a lot of&nbsp; other drugs. And that’s part of the appeal. The GLP 1 drug class has been on the market&nbsp; for 15 years so there is a well-established&nbsp;&nbsp; safety database, so I think it’s&nbsp; a pretty safe drug class for sure. So far, Jackie’s tolerated the injections well. I've had no negative side effects,&nbsp;&nbsp; which was something that really scared me&nbsp; to begin with. It seems to be doing the job. I'm not quite on the full dose&nbsp; yet, that's to come in the future,&nbsp;&nbsp; but so far I seems to be&nbsp; tolerating it really well. So how are social media influencers&nbsp; and celebrities getting their hands on&nbsp;these drugs&nbsp; if they’re intended for those&nbsp; with diabetes or struggling with obesity? The answer lies in a practice&nbsp; called ‘off label’ prescriptions. This is when a drug is used for a&nbsp; medical condition that it is not&nbsp;&nbsp; approved to treat or given in a different&nbsp; way or in a different dose than intended. The practice is commonly used in cancer treatment. Jackie has been taking Ozempic, off-label,&nbsp;&nbsp; as Wegovy was only approved for use in&nbsp; the UK’s free health care system in 2023. I have yo-yo-ed so much. And ever since taking this, I've been consistent. Which has been awesome, mentally and physically. And it's been working. I've lost. I've lost weight. As the drugs become more popular, concerns about&nbsp;off-label prescriptions are on the rise too. Novo, understandably, can’t stop Elon Musk&nbsp; from sending a Tweet out to his followers. It’s out of their control. What they&nbsp; can do is control their sales force to&nbsp;&nbsp; make sure that the messaging is all as per label. And I think it’s worth noting&nbsp; that Novo have yet to actually&nbsp;&nbsp; do any direct-to-consumer advertising in the US. Anyone who brings up TikTok, Instagram and&nbsp; puts WeGovy and sees what’s going on there, I don’t think Novo would condone what’s&nbsp; going on in terms of off-label promotion. I think there's a fine line between prescribing&nbsp; off label and prescribing totally against the&nbsp;&nbsp; advice of the manufacturer and of all the&nbsp; other organizations that create guidelines. I don't think there'll be a huge issue in terms of people taking it outside of the recommended&nbsp; BMI ranges, at least not legitimately. CNBC’s Meg Tirrell asked Novo Nordisk CEO&nbsp; about the ‘off label’ use of its drugs. We’re always concerned about, you&nbsp;know, the safety of our medicines&nbsp;&nbsp; and that’s why we really focus&nbsp; on promoting it the right way,&nbsp;&nbsp; getting it to a physician and describing&nbsp; how our products should be used. There is also one big known catch:&nbsp; if a patient stops taking the drugs,&nbsp;&nbsp; they are likely to regain most of the weight lost. This means a patient might end up injecting&nbsp; for life if they want to keep the weight off. How do you feel about the fact that you know,&nbsp; there's a possibility that if you stop taking&nbsp;&nbsp; the treatment that you might put some of the&nbsp; weight or all of the weight back on? Yeah. Oh, I mean, that scares me, and it’s somewhere&nbsp; I've been as well. I’ve sat here lighter. I think this drug is so new,&nbsp; that perhaps maybe down the line,&nbsp;&nbsp; it might be something where they say it's&nbsp; something you need to take long term. WeGovy can cost over $1,000 a&nbsp; month in the U.S. out of pocket,&nbsp;&nbsp; so affordability is a big barrier for these drugs. Analysts do expect these costs to come down&nbsp; over time as more versions enter the market. If insurers and public health&nbsp; institutions can be convinced that the drugs reduce the risk of long-term&nbsp; weight-related complications like&nbsp;&nbsp; cardiovascular disease, they might&nbsp; be inclined to help foot the bill. There’s a very strong or very&nbsp; important study looking at WeGovy&nbsp;&nbsp; in 17,000 non-diabetics, but obese patients. Its primary aim is to look at the&nbsp; cardiovascular benefit of WeGovy,&nbsp;&nbsp; so does it reduce the number&nbsp; of strokes and heart attacks. Now if that trial is positive, I&nbsp;think that would add fuel to the fire. It also opens the door to what’s&nbsp; going to happen in Medicare. Beyond safety and cost, there is concern that&nbsp; if drugs like these are used by individuals with&nbsp;&nbsp; more modest weight loss goals, it could take the&nbsp; body positivity movement in the wrong direction. The finance community is exuberant&nbsp; about the potential of these drugs. Novo Nordisk’s share price has doubled&nbsp; over the last two years and still,&nbsp;&nbsp; healthcare analysts think it&nbsp; could go higher from here. There’s no reason why the GLP-1 market in&nbsp; diabetes alone couldn’t be worth $40bn+ over time. Then we move to GLP-1 in obesity.&nbsp; Novo is the only show in town today. They generated over $2 billion of sales in 2022. Ultimately, this isn’t just about one company. While Novo Nordisk has been the leader,&nbsp; Eli Lilly, Pfizer and more are all hoping&nbsp;&nbsp; to get a piece of the pie with a whole host&nbsp; of promising obesity drugs in the pipeline. Around the world, it doesn't matter&nbsp; whether it's North America, Europe,&nbsp;&nbsp; Asia, you're seeing phenomenal growth. The current script trends are ridiculous. I absolutely think this is a unique drug&nbsp; class, and that Lilly and Novo will probably&nbsp;&nbsp; have two of the biggest selling drugs that&nbsp; we've ever seen across diabetes, obesity. I’ve never seen anything like&nbsp; this, no, in my 20 years.

moving on to the medication it is called ozempic as we discussed and it comes in a box like this and it also comes with a box of needles that are used to inject the medicine uh into our body so to use the pen the pen already comes pre-filled with the medicine which is a clear liquid which we have to make sure that it is clear before we use it and it is used along with the needle so here is how we use the pen so we remove this plastic wrap from behind the needle and we screw the needle onto the pen like this after that we take the big cap out and we also take the smaller cap out the big cap we will use later to remove the needle from the pen after we have injected the medication so how does the dosing work this pen has 0.25 milligram or 0.5 milligram dose delivery options there's another pen which comes which delivers one milligram which is a higher dose so in the beginning your doctor might tell you to deliver a lower dose so we are going to talk about that so when we use a new pen out of the box then for the first time we have to use what we call is priming the pen so we have to go to this marker here which has two dots and we have to make sure that we push it out and we see a drop in in the pen to make sure that the the pen and the medication and the needle are communicating and the pen is working we do not have to do this step at subsequent injections with the same pen we always do it for the first time now to deliver the actual dose we go again we look at the doors window here and we go to the first usually the first lowest stores which is 0.25 milligrams we dial that we already have our needle in place and what we do is we usually inject it in our belly in our abdomen and it has to be at least two finger bridges away from the belly button it could be more as well so it has to be at least one inch away from the belly button to the sides of the abdomen and then what we do is we do not have to pinch the skin of the belly we go straight in like this 90 degrees we press the injector and then hold for five seconds and remove the medication and the pen so it is a very straightforward simple process and the dosing works usually the way the dosing works is that in the first four weeks you use the of 0.25 milligram once a week remember this pen this dose is usually used once a week not more frequently than that so you have to set up a reminder to use it on a certain day of the week it can be with or without a meal and after four weeks if your doctor has recommended then you might be asked to increase the dose to 0.5 milligram once a week however this depends on the discretion of your health care provider so after we are done with the injection we have to use the big cap we put it on the pen push it all the way in and unscrew it so that the needle and the cap come out and this has to be disposed into a sharps container not the regular garbage so we keep that away safely and we close the pen which can be stored at room temperature for up to eight weeks so that was how to use an ozempic pen for management of diabetes thank you

- Tips to Help Manage Your #Ozempic Side Effects: Kind: captions Language: en tips to reduce ozempic side effects [Music] first off a disclaimer this is not intended as medical advice and it's not personalized to the individual patient so if you are experiencing side effects from your medication please speak to your prescribing physician so quick recap some agletide is the medication that is marketed and branded as ozempic at one milligram a week for diabetes and at 2.4 milligrams a week for weight loss branded as way Govi in this video let in general let's talk about some aglotide so the most common side effects with this medication are gastrointestinal or GI and these can include nausea constipation diarrhea gas and bloating and abdominal pain the number one thing you can do to help reduce these side effects is to cut back on your portion sizes most of my patients report they eat about half the portion sizes they were eating before and if you eat beyond the point of feeling full you're going to feel quite sick to your stomach so eat very slowly and stop as soon as you feel full in terms of how to change up your daily pattern of eating again most my patients do find that eating smaller more frequent meals throughout the day as opposed to a large meal at the end of the day can help reduce their symptoms I often remind them though that nausea can be a result of overeating but it can also be caused by leaving it too long between your meals so I always advise my patients to pack a small healthy snack to have on hand when they're out and about in case they start to feel nauseated or lightheaded when it's been too long since they last ate in terms of what foods to eat it's best to avoid foods that can cause gas things like beans and broccoli and artificial sweeteners and it's best to avoid very high fat foods and foods that are really high in sugar in terms of when to do your injection if you have a predictable work schedule say Monday to Friday I often tell my patients try injecting Friday night right before bed because if you're going to feel nauseated it does tend to be in the day or two after the injection and if you're at home and don't have to go to work that can make that more manageable some of my patients also report that injecting Smeg latid in their thigh as opposed to their abdominal area can help produce specifically the gastrointestinal side effects it's also really important to drink lots of water when you're on this medication because that can also help reduce the symptoms of constipation and remember that if you're having side effects early on the majority of them do resolve after one or two months as your body gets used to this medication but remember if you're still having concerning side effects best to speak to your physician because they can recommend over-the-counter remedies that can help make them more manageable or they might switch up and slow down your titration schedule or how quickly you increase the dose to help reduce side effects hope that helps

- You're Being Lied To About Ozempic | Truth Complex | Business Insider: Kind: captions Language: en-US In just a few years, Ozempic has become a household name and a cultural phenomenon. GLP-1 drugs are everywhere, and already one in eight US adults have tried them. The Danish company that makes Ozempic is worth more than The Coca-Cola Company and McDonald's combined. Look online, and you'll see comments about "Ozempic face," hair loss, severe stomach problems, and dangerous side effects. And then a moment later you'll see a report that it could help with fertility or treating Alzheimer's or even shopping addiction. And it's not just weight-loss drugs. We've been lied to about weight loss itself for years now. How much do we know about GLP-1 drugs? And are we being told the full truth about them? I've spent the last few months diving into the research and even tried to get some GLP-1 medication myself to see what's really going on here. At this point, I spent a lot of time on social media digesting what the discourse is out there about this medication. One of the pieces of misinformation I'm seeing over and over again is this idea that these drugs are brand new, that they're not well studied, and that they're not well understood. It's not studied for this. You know, there's no studies on what it does to a body without diabetes. There is no research, no research, on a person that is not diagnosed with type 2 diabetes. So, we know that this line that they're untested or not understood is just not true. They've been studied a lot for both safety and efficacy for both diabetes and weight loss. Thousands of people have been studied in clinical trials specifically for obesity but not for diabetes. The new phenomenon is that Ozempic is a household name, and it became a household name because of its weight-loss effects. However, it's part of a class of drugs that have been used for decades to help people with type 2 diabetes regulate their blood sugar. And just in that regard, it's been a huge medical breakthrough. I think we could probably critique that on its own in that our society is so weight-obsessed that none of these diabetes treatments were a household name even though a million and a half people die of it each year, but as soon as it started making celebrities skinny, all of us were talking about it. That's probably worth unpacking, but that's more than we will have time to do here. So, when Novo Nordisk started testing these drugs as an obesity treatment, the outcome of those trials suggested that these are the most effective weight-loss drugs ever. So, the main ingredient in Ozempic is semaglutide. It's part of a class of drugs called GLP-1 receptor agonists, which basically just means they mimic a hormone that naturally occurs in your body called GLP-1. Semaglutide is in both Ozempic and Wegovy, but Wegovy is the one that's FDA-approved for weight loss. If these medications are paired with a healthy diet and exercise, people who are on semaglutide lost, on average, 15% of their body weight. The people who were just dieting and exercising lost, on average, 2.4% A newer drug made by Eli Lilly called tirzepatide did even better in its clinical trials, with people losing an average of 20% of their body weight. And so based on that data, we started to have all this very hyped news coverage about how these drugs were a game changer and a blockbuster and how maybe obesity would be cured forever, which honestly is kind of a bizarre framing. Even from the very first studies, there were people who were not responding to these drugs. Yes, it's a really big deal that 86% of people in a trial lost a significant amount of weight. However, that means 14% of people didn't. Despite all this talk about miracle drugs, the figures are basically the same for both semaglutide and tirzepatide. Roughly one in 10 people don't lose significant weight while on this medication and are what's called nonresponders. I really wanted to speak to someone who went through this, so I put a survey up on BI's social-media channels, and that's how I found Erika. It's working for other people. Why isn't it working for me and my body chemistry? I'm mad it didn't work, but then it just, I accept it, and I just got to find another way to lose the weight. Erika says her doctor didn't warn her that these drugs don't work for everybody. Like many of us, most of her information came mainly from media coverage. When you set these drugs up as a miracle or a magic pill, you're also setting people up for massive disappointment if they had high hopes of weight loss and then these drugs don't work for them. There is one more thing that a lot of coverage has missed. Real life is not the same as a clinical trial. Since those first trials and all that news coverage, there's been more data about what real-world usage looks like. A 2023 study followed people on semaglutide for 52 weeks, and those people lost an average of 4.4% of their body weight. So we went from 15% down to 4%, which is a dramatic difference. Most of the early coverage of these drugs used the numbers from the clinical trials, which makes sense because it was the first time these drugs were studied. The subsequent real-world-usage impacts have not gotten as much press coverage, and that definitely feeds into an inflated sense of how revolutionary these drugs could be. Certainly, they are a really big deal. They're more effective than prior drugs, but there are limitations. The other thing that I think might be getting lost here is that weight loss from these drugs will have a plateau, which is similar to diet, to exercise, and even to bariatric surgery. At some point, every weight-loss intervention plateaus. There's this popular conception that losing weight is the hard part and maintaining weight loss should be easy. What we know is that it's actually the opposite, right? The maintenance of weight loss is actually the hardest part. You wouldn't want a drug that allows you to lose unlimited amounts of weight. That would be dangerous, for one, and then you'd have to go off of it when you reached your goal weight, and then you would just start to climb back up again, and it would be this constant yo-yo. However, that doesn't stop people from talking about this plateau like it's some sort of betrayal of the drug or some sort of failure of this medication. All evidence points to needing to keep taking these drugs to keep benefiting from them, which is interesting because we have study data showing that most people don't keep taking them. One study looked at thousands of people with GLP-1 prescriptions and found that about half of them stopped taking it after a year, and 70% of them stopped taking it at the two-year mark. And another study found that after stopping the medication, people regained, on average, two-thirds of the weight that they'd lost. This is perhaps another big reason to dial down the hype machine on these medications. Yes, they are a big breakthrough medically, but if people don't want to be on them, then the impact of the drugs will be much lower than were predicted in the flurry of news coverage we had to begin with. A lot of people hear that this is a long-term, potentially lifelong, medication, and they see big pharma dollar signs. The cynic in all of us sort of wakes up and says, "What a win for big pharma." It's true that it is a win for big pharma. They're probably going to make a ton of money on this, and there is absolutely no denying that. I understand why people are mistrusting of pharmaceutical companies and the media coverage around them. At this point, we've seen some very irresponsible reporting. I think it's really important to be clear-eyed about the role of big pharmaceutical companies in our society. With just a quick Google, you can see that Novo Nordisk has paid fines after being accused of shady sales tactics, they've faced criticism for spending loads of money wining and dining doctors, and that they've gotten in trouble with regulators in multiple countries. More famously, Novo Nordisk, along with other drugmakers, faced massive criticism for high insulin prices. Nowadays, insulin is less profitable than it once was, but the potential profits of weight-loss drugs remain astronomical. And make no mistake, Novo Nordisk will be cashing in on these drugs and already is. At one point, the company's valuation was bigger than the entire GDP of Denmark. Understandably, some people who don't trust big pharma or simply don't have access to these drugs are turning to natural alternatives. Let's talk a little bit more about nature's Ozempic. Nature's Ozempic. You'll find people presenting berberine, peppermint tea, green tea, vitamin B12, high-fiber diets, high-protein diets, all of these things as being natural alternatives to taking Ozempic. There are certain foods that can increase the amount of GLP-1 hormone that your body makes. The part where they get it wrong is that that doesn't necessarily equate to weight loss, which appears to be the goal of many people consuming this content. The level of GLP-1 production that diets can provide is nowhere near what you can achieve with prescription medications. These interventions are leading to supraphysiological levels of GLP-1, as opposed to modest changes in your diet or some supplement that you could take. These are just orders of magnitude different, and you're not going to see anywhere near the same effects. But between influencer nutritionists and supplement grifters, we're seeing tremendous amounts of misinformation online about how effective a GLP-1-boosting diet can be. These foods and vitamins are generally less risky than a serious prescription drug, but they're almost definitely less effective as well. This is another subject of intensive news coverage where I think we just need to turn the hype dial down a little bit and look at what the research actually tells us. I also think people pay a lot of attention to side effects of these drugs because we've had plenty of examples of prescription drugs coming onto the market and a side effect no one knew about ends up being very serious, particularly for weight-loss drugs. The most common side effects of these medications are gastrointestinal, things like nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. These can seem a little bit trivial, just like tummy trouble or whatever, but they can be quite serious. Extended periods of vomiting or diarrhea can leave you dangerously dehydrated. It's even been reported that one woman died while taking these drugs after months of gastrointestinal issues, including severe vomiting and abdominal pain. Besides the GI issues, the FDA label for these drugs includes a number of other warnings, some of which are for very serious conditions. So if we just pull up the label now, there is what's called a black-box warning about the risk of thyroid C-cell tumors. The warning label mentions findings in rodents, but a GLP-1 researcher I spoke to said that the thyroid in rodents and humans is quite different. So those findings probably aren't relevant. Sometimes by putting a warning label on because of initial good intentions, you can cause more harm by scaring away people who otherwise might be willing to take this medicine and benefit from the medicine. While the link between GLP-1 medication and thyroid cancer has been hard to establish, the link between obesity and thyroid cancer is much clearer. One study found that for every five-point increase in BMI, the risk of thyroid cancer increases by 30%. There's also another category on this label called warnings and precautions, and it includes acute pancreatitis, acute gallbladder disease, hypoglycemia, acute kidney injury, hypersensitivity reactions, diabetic retinopathy, heart-rate increase, and suicidal behavior and ideation. The label here just says: "Monitor for depression or suicidal thoughts. Discontinue Wegovy if symptoms develop." That's not very reassuring to me. This is an ongoing and evolving thing, but the weight of evidence so far suggests that these drugs alone are not going to cause you to have suicidal thoughts. It is not uncommon for drugs to have very intimidating, if not downright scary-looking, FDA warning labels. It's important to remember that things listed on these labels are potential risks rather than guaranteed results, which actually does seem to get lost in the shuffle a bit on social media. Most of the more serious side effects of GLP-1 drugs are on the rare side. Most people who take these medications will not get pancreatitis. Sometimes people wonder why a drug could be approved if it has so many alarming side effects, but it's really not uncommon. If you look at metformin, a very popular diabetes medication, it has a warning for potentially fatal lactic acidosis. If you look at the label for ibuprofen, it has a warning about potential increased risk of stroke. These things are calculated in the context of being sick. If you take these drugs and don't have a medical need for them, your risk calculation is different and you are putting yourself at greater risk of harm. Your neighbor who is interested in taking this medicine to lose 10 pounds so they can get into a size-eight dress for Uncle Harry's wedding, they weren't studied in a clinical trial. But besides what's on the label, which is scary enough on its own, there's all these rumors on social media about a whole other category of effects that are mostly cosmetic, things like "Ozempic face" or hair loss. And it's not just hair loss. There's been a ton of concern about how much muscle people lose while taking GLP-1 drugs. However, whenever you lose weight, even if it's just through diet and exercise, you do tend to lose some lean muscle. One thing that's often forgotten in this whole conversation is that weight loss itself can have some very serious side effects, particularly rapid or extreme weight loss. Even losing weight fast through diet has been linked to hair loss, muscle loss, depression, gallstones -- all side effects that overlap with the side effects of Ozempic. So it's really hard to tell where one ends and the other begins. Personally, I think this is just another example of how the conversation around Ozempic has just gotten really weird. People seem to want there to be a punishment for taking a vanity drug and almost gleefully report this idea that your face or your hair or whatever would fail you when you've taken a step to make your body look nicer. At least, that's the perception. And to be sure, some people have taken Ozempic as a vanity drug. These drugs became a household name because celebrities were touting them for weight loss, and in many cases, already-thin celebrities were touting their weight-loss benefits. So, we've talked about the bad, but there are also some really interesting positive effects that these drugs potentially have. Not only do they make you crave less food, they make you desire different, usually healthier food. There's a physiological response that causes some people to be more preoccupied with food than others. People I talk to who have been suffering from food noise say the drug feels like a switch is being flipped in their brains. People have now reported other cravings going away too, like the urge to online shop. These drugs could help with all kinds of conditions -- Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, alcohol addiction, and other types of addiction, depression. There's even anecdotal reports of Ozempic babies, which are people whose fertility improved while they were on these drugs. All these things aren't so much side effects as they are potentially brand-new indications for the drugs, brand-new conditions that they could treat. GLP-1 drugs have a really unusually high number of potential new indications. About one in eight Americans have already tried GLP-1 medications, and this explosion of interest has led to something we've never quite seen before. One of the strangest elements of this rush for everyone to try semaglutide is that it put the drug in shortage. And when drugs are on the official FDA shortage list, compounding pharmacies can make their own versions. Compounded versions of these drugs are not FDA-approved or -reviewed, and because these pharmacies are regulated at the state level, there's no data available for how many people are taking compounded GLP-1s. In an environment this confusing, it becomes easier to take advantage of people. I spoke to a researcher who purposefully ordered compounded semaglutide from shady online sellers just to see what he would get. We looked at the online pharmacies that were no prescription and were highest ranked in search results. So these were some of the websites that consumers will see first. Of the six drugs that he ordered, three never came at all. They were total scams. So they took the three prescriptions that they actually received and put the drugs to the test, and what they found was evidence of contamination and improper amounts of the active ingredient, semaglutide. The FDA has also issued a warning about some compounders using semaglutide salt, which is generally used only for research and is not approved for use in humans. So, for consumers, the most important thing is to understand that when you source it from a no-prescription online pharmacy, there's a lot of risks. Either fraud, incorrect product, or, again, you're not getting a product that is medically necessary for you or appropriate for you. Offering no-prescription versions of prescription drugs is illegal, but there's also what feels like a tidal wave of buzzy online health startups who can temporarily sell totally legally. I actually went through the process of being prescribed compounded semaglutide. I went through an online pharmacy where all I had to do was fill out a survey and then do a video telehealth visit with a doctor to be prescribed these drugs. I went through this process for reporting purposes to see how easy it would be to get these drugs, and it turns out it was pretty easy, which, depending how much you value drug regulation, could be kind of concerning. There was sort of no question that I was going to get these drugs. From the second the call started, it was a given. I don't know why, but I thought he might challenge me and say, like, "Ah, these are some pretty heavy-duty side effects. Are you sure you want to take these drugs to lose a small amount of weight?" But he didn't. And the doctor that I spoke to in that process did not give any indication that I would need to stay on these drugs to maintain the weight loss. I'm not saying all this to fearmonger about compounding pharmacies, but it is kind of wild how little we know about how many people are taking drugs from compounding pharmacies when we do know that they come with a higher degree of risk. However, there is one considerable upside, and that's that for some people, compounding pharmacies offer a cheaper version of these drugs. In my case, a month's supply of semaglutide cost $250. The list price for Ozempic in the United States is almost $1,000 for a month's supply. For Wegovy, it's $1,349. Those are shocking price tags. You can find economists estimating that GLP-1 medications will add $1 trillion in healthcare spending to the US. Then we have other economists estimating that it's actually somewhere between 20% and 50% of that. And it's not really possible to know who's right or wrong because the actual prices paid to drugmakers are secret. And that's one of the many oddities of the American healthcare system, is that no one knows exactly what drugmakers actually make from selling their drugs. It's considered proprietary information. At the end of the day, a lot of people seem to want it to be true that these drugs could actually save healthcare systems money, that we spend so much on treating obesity and related conditions that these drugs could actually be a net money saver. And while that might eventually happen, especially if the cost of these drugs goes down, that is a very rare feat for a drug to pull off. The most important thing to remember is that if you see estimates of how much a certain drug is going to cost US healthcare systems or how much obesity costs America in general, remember that these are really rough estimates. And that's important because the stigma that people with obesity face is often couched in this idea that they're a tremendous financial burden to the healthcare system. I have seen countless comments saying, "Why do you need Ozempic if you can just eat healthier?" "Why do you need to take these drugs?" "What you're looking for is willpower." It simply doesn't make sense to talk about Ozempic as being the easy way out. The easy way out implies there's another way out, and for a lot of people, there just isn't. What these medicines have taught us is that a huge number of people living with obesity, they were restricting their calories, they were following diets, they were exercising, and it just didn't work for them even though they really wanted it to work. And now we have something that helps them, and guess what? They're losing weight. We've been lied to about weight loss for a long time, and we're only just starting to figure it out. The reality is that we, both as a culture and the medical community, have been significantly underplaying how difficult it is to lose weight and especially how difficult it is to maintain weight loss. We have a ton of data on this at this point, and we know that drastic weight loss is exceedingly rare and is usually temporary. Over time, we've also learned that exercise is not nearly as big of a factor in weight loss as people tend to suggest. It has many benefits and is still important for overall health, but burning more calories is not a guaranteed path to weight loss. Many studies have shown at this point that even in the best of circumstances, where we're combining diet, exercise, and behavioral counseling, people tend to only lose 5% to 10% of their body weight. Then, after that, your body fights back. When you lose weight, your body starts to release hormones that increase appetite and reduce feelings of fullness. So there's actually a biological mechanism encouraging people to eat more as weight is lost. Studies on people who've lost quite a lot of weight have found that they burn fewer calories than scientists expected, even when you account for the fact that their bodies are smaller and they're carrying less weight around. If you lose roughly 20 pounds or so and you're maintaining that 20-pound weight loss, you're probably burning about 250 calories per day less than you were before you lost the weight. And we've known that for a very long time. The advice that doctors gave for years was that all it takes is small tweaks to your lifestyle. Swap your bag of chips for an apple or take the stairs. You can still find all kinds of advice like that online. And it's actually, frankly, bizarre that after decades of research supporting the idea that this is a very complex issue, so many people insist on acting like it's easy. It is understandable to have anxiety about medicating millions of people for what's seen as a wider societal issue. And some people say this is a Band-Aid on a bigger problem, those bigger problems being things like our food system or our sedentary lifestyles. And, yes, there would likely be tremendous social benefit to addressing those very complex systemic problems, but for now, we have a relatively safe and effective drug that can give people another tool to manage their health in the meantime. So maybe it's a Band-Aid, but sometimes you just need a Band-Aid. If you want to see the sources I used for this video, check out the link in the video description. And for more "Truth Complex," subscribe to Business Insider.

- What to Eat on GLP-1 to Stop Side Effects (2026 Nutritionist Guide) #GLP1Diet: Kind: captions Language: en Are you tired of feeling like your stomach is in a knot every time you take your shot? You started this medication to feel better and get healthy, but now the nausea, the break in your stomach, and the constant exhaustion are making it hard to even get through the day. As a public health nutritionist, I see this every single day. Most people think these side effects are just part of the deal. But I'm here to tell you that what you put on your plate can be the difference between a miserable week and a successful journey. Today we are going to talk about exactly what to eat to quiet those side effects. No fancy diets, no offlimits lists, just real food for real relief. Stick around to the end because I'm sharing my safe play checklist that you can use every single day. Let's keep it simple. These medications work by slowing down your stomach. Think of your digestion like a highway. Usually cars move through at a steady pace, but GLP1 puts a slow sign up. The cars stay on the road longer. This is great for weight loss because you stay full, but if you keep trying to send more cars down that road or if the cars are big, heavy trucks like greasy or spicy foods, you get a massive traffic jam. That jam is what causes the nausea and bloating. When you eat less, every bite counts more. If you aren't eating the right things, your body starts to run on low battery. This isn't just about weight. It's about your energy and your muscles. Today, we fix that. Let's focus on our first fix, quieting nausea. Nausea is the most common complaint. When your stomach feels like it's rolling, stay away from hot, strong smelling foods. Cold or room temperature foods are much easier to handle. Try air dried proteins like cold turkey slices or hard-boiled eggs. If you feel a wave of nausea, try three small sips of ginger tea and a plain cracker. Wait 10 minutes before trying anything else. Avoid the big three triggers. Fried foods, heavy creams, and spicy peppers. They stay in your stomach way too long and make that traffic jam worse. Next up, let's look at beating constipation. Constipation happens because your gut is moving slowly and soaking up all the water. Think of fiber like a broom and water like the bucket of sads. You need both to clean the floors. If you eat fiber but don't drink water, that fiber just becomes a bigger break. Try gentle fiber like cooked carrots, oats, or berries. Raw, crunchy veggies can sometimes be too tough when you're first starting out. Cook them until they are soft. And as always, please consult your doctor before making any major changes. Fatigue is a major factor with GLP-1 medications. If you feel like you're walking through mud, you likely need more protein. When you lose weight fast, your body wants to burn muscle for energy. Aim for protein first at every meal. Greek yogurt, cottage cheese, and tofu are great because they are easy to eat even if you aren't very hungry. Your stomach is smaller now. Big meals are the enemy. Try five tiny power snacks throughout the day instead. Stop eating the very second you feel a sigh of fullness. If you wait until you're stuffed, it's too late. Dehydration makes side effects worse. But remember, large gulps can cause nausea. Sip. Don't gulp. If plain water tastes off, add a slice of cucumber or lemon. Keep a bottle with you for tiny sips all day. Here is your checklist. Eat protein first. Keep meals fist sized. and sip water between meals, not during them. I want you to stay safe. If you can't keep water down for 24 hours or have severe pain, call your doctor immediately. You are the boss of your body. Always keep your doctor in the loop about how you're feeling. What food has been a lifesaver for you? Let us know in the comments. Like and subscribe for more nutrition tips.

- The GLP 1 Paradox:  Lose Weight ....But at what cost? #healthnexus #weightloss #ozempic #orthopedics: Kind: captions Language: en GLP1s, your muscles, joints, and bones. Game changers if used correctly. Hi folks, I'm Dr. Lyall Ashberg, your friendly orthopedic surgeon. Today I'd like to talk to you about GLP1s and their relationship to orthopedics and muscular skeletal health. Glucagon-l like peptide 1 receptor agonists or GLP1s including medications like Zanic, WGOI, Zepbound are transforming weight loss across the world. But here's the question you and your clinicians should be asking. What are they doing to your muscles, your bones, and your joints? And are you getting healthier? Or are you just getting lighter? Today, I'm going to break down what the science actually shows and more importantly, how to use these medications the right way to protect your body and to optimize your performance. Why does this matter? As an orthopedic surgeon, I don't just care about your weight. What I care about more is what that weight is made of. To be a little more technical, what is your body composition? Or put another way, what percentage of your body is muscle, fat, water, or bone? Because that determines how you move, how you feel, and how you age. And obesity and diabetes are not just metabolic diseases. They are muscularkeeletal diseases. And they've been shown to increase your infection risk, delay tissue healing, and lead to more complications after surgery. They can affect your bone and muscle quality and promote an overall inflammatory state. So when we talk about GLP1s, we're not just talking about weight loss. We're talking about changing the trajectory of your entire muscular skeletal system. GP1 medications work through several mechanisms. They suppress appetite. They slow gastric emptying, leaving you fuller longer. They improve insulin glucose dynamics and they act on the brain to increase satiety. So patients eat less and they lose weight up to 15 to 20% of their body weight or more depending on the medication and the dose. But here's a key point. These medications don't decide what kind of weight you lose. That decision is driven by how you live. So let's start with muscle. Some studies show that up to 30 to 40% of the weight lost on GLP1 therapy can actually come from lean mass of which muscle is a major player. Now lean mass is not just muscle. It actually includes muscle, bone, water and your organ tissue. But muscle is a major component and that matters because muscle is largely responsible for your body's metabolic currency which is in the form of ATP or the dollars most of which is made by your mitochondria the bank and it determines your functional capacity and your longevity reserve. So if you lose too much muscle, you may be lighter, but you may also be weaker. Now let's talk about bone. When you lose weight rapidly, your skeleton experiences less load. And importantly, bone adapts to load. So what happens when your bone sees less stress or weight in this instance? Your bone density may go down. And this is a physiologic response sometimes referred to as the mechanoat theory. And if this is not addressed, it could potentially increase your long-term risk for fractures and structural decline. So clinically, what does this mean? You can lose weight and simultaneously move towards sarcopenia or loss of muscle mass, which may lead to frailty and a higher injury risk. This is the paradox. But wait, there's more. It's important to note that what I've told you so far is not the whole story. GLP1s have some very important positive effects. As I'm sure many of you are aware, chronic inflammation, which is typical of so many of the illnesses we see in our society, including metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and some people with obesity, is one of the root causes affecting so many of our body systems, including our muscularkeeletal system. But GLP1s are among other things strongly anti-inflammatory. And so they reduce cytoines which are the chemicals which mediate inflammation including things like TNF alpha interlucan 6 and interlucan 1 beta. When it comes to the muscularkeeletal system let's take a look at each system a little bit closer. They affect joint biology directly and have a whole joint effect. So we now know that GLP-1 receptors exist in cartilage, the tissue that lines the ends of your bone, and synovium, the tissue that lines the joint capsule, and is responsible for nourishing our joints. Which means these medications may actually modulate osteoarthritis or arthritis progression by decreasing the inflammation in joints, protecting cartilage cells from premature death or epoptosis and facilitating cartilage matrix formation. All of these may enable your joints to live longer. On the muscle side, while total lean body mass may decrease, muscle quality may actually improve. And so we see things like reduced fatty infiltration of muscle cells, improved mitochondrial function, and better metabolic efficiency. So again, less muscle, but potentially better muscle. On the bone side, there are signals that GLP1s may actually increase osteoblasts, the bone forming cells activity, and reduce osteoplasts, the bone resorbing cells signaling. So they actually may partially offset potential bone loss from stress or the lack of stress related weight reduction. Therefore, overall, GLP1s are probably beneficial in that they improve both muscle and bone quality and therefore there's a net positive to our overall muscularkeeletal system function in that they decrease the risk of falls and they present an opportunity. TLP-1 therapy is not a solution by itself. It's a tool and used incorrectly, it can lead to a loss of muscle and bone. But used correctly, it becomes one of the most powerful metabolic interventions we have. So, how do we do this correctly? This is where structure matters. Number one, exercise. This is non-negotiable. If you're on a GLP1 and not doing resistance training, you're making a mistake. Exercise by itself is probably the tool with the most bang for the buck that you can get from nearly any intervention, especially when you're taking GLP1s. So, what does this look like? Well, resistance training for muscle and bone. You should think about doing this two to three times per week minimum. And this has been shown to reduce muscle loss dramatically in some studies up to 50 to 90% in patients who are on GLP1s. Next, you want to work on and add aerobic work for your cardiovascular fitness and longevity. I would shoot for 150 minutes per week of moderate zone 2 for those of you know about it or 75 minutes of vigorous activity per week, obviously divided. If you've not been doing any exercise at all, start slowly and build progressively. Rome wasn't built in a day. Don't hurt yourself. And most of all, make it sustainable, something that you can keep up. Start with movement, then resistance, and then start thinking about performance. Number two, diet. This should be individualized, but here are some important guidelines for folks on GLP1s. Protein strategy. Protein is essential. Again, obviously, this varies, but you should try and target 1.2 to 1.6 g per kilogram per day. And most patients under consume protein on GLP-1 therapy. Why? Because they're not hungry. So this needs to be intentional. Eat protein first. And you should use dense sources like eggs, fatty fish, Greek yogurts, lean meats, tofu, protein supplements if you need to. Make sure you're hydrating. Hydration matters. At least 2 to three lers of water per day. And one other thing and a key mistake is unintentional fasting. People skip meals because they're not hungry and lose muscle as a result. Satiety is a tool, but it must be managed. If you're not measuring, you're guessing. Use or ask your doctor about a body impedance analysis or scale for frequent tracking. or consider getting a DEXA periodity or an MRI if you've got the bucks for it. And some simple things you can do include strength metrics like grip strength, functional capacity, i.e. how easily can you go up steps or pick up heavier things. Watching your weight is not enough from a surgical standpoint. GLP-1s may actually reduce your infection risk, improve tissue healing, improve surgical outcomes in some patients, and potentially delay the need for joint replacement. And they improve your overall cardiac, renal, and respiratory risk profile, but they also introduce some important peroperative considerations. Delayed gastric emptying can actually increase your potential for aspiration. especially when under anesthesia. So, always tell your surgeon or your anesthesiologist if you've been taking GLP1s and you're having surgery. GLP1s are powerful, but not for everyone in the same way. Higher risk groups include older patients, patients with low baseline muscle mass, people with poor nutrition, and it is possible to be overweight and undernourished. and sedentary individuals who have poorer muscle mass. These patients need structure. The big takeaway, GLP-1 medications in and of themselves don't make you healthy, but they do create an amazing opportunity to become healthier. So, you're not just losing weight, you're actually remodeling your body, your muscle, your bones, your metabolism, and your function. and you need to guide that process or you may not like the outcome. If you're considering GLP1 therapy or you are currently on it, you need a structured sustainable plan to change your whole human health. And that's exactly what we do at Health Nexus Orthopedics and Wellness. If this was helpful, please subscribe and share it with someone considering these medications. And stay tuned because this is just the beginning. Let's get you living your best life. I look forward to seeing you. Cheers.
