Yeah. >> But the data is actually there. >> It's totally wild. So, today we aren't just talking about, you know, living longer. We're really getting into hacking the biological mechanisms that control how well you live. We're doing a deep dive into a huge stack of research, mostly anchored by the text, the longevity blueprint, vitality for brain and body, and we are pulling out the absolute most critical evidence-based stuff you can actually use. >> Right. And the key here, the really important part is understanding the mechanisms. It's um it's not enough to just copy some protocol you saw on social media. Yes. Because when you understand exactly why a habit works on a cellular level, like how it actually interacts with your nervous system or, you know, your mitochondria, it transforms from just a daily chore into this targeted lever you can pull to change your biology. >> I love that. A targeted lever. Okay, so let's just jump right into the data. Um, starting with metabolic health because there's this statistic in the sources about continuous glucose monitors that honestly it literally made me stop and reread the page. >> Oh, the sleep one. Yes, >> like we all know sleep is important, short, but the data shows that just one single night of bad sleep induces this state of temporary insulin resistance that is so severe your next day glucose levels can jump by almost 40%. >> Yeah, 40%. >> 40%. Just from tossing and turning in bed. I mean, how does that even happen? >> What's fascinating here is the underlying hormonal cascade, right? That 40% spike. It isn't just a random glitch. When you get uh a fragmented night of sleep, your body basically perceives that as a serious threat, >> like a survival thing. >> Exactly. It drives up your sympathetic nervous system, so your fight orflight response, and it just spikes your cortisol. And high cortisol basically screams at your liver to start aggressively dumping all its stored glucose into your bloodstream to prepare for an emergency, right? While simultaneously making your cells totally resistant to insulin. So your body is essentially barricading the doors to your cells, trapping all that sugar right there in your blood, >> which is, you know, a massive problem if you're waking up and about to eat just a normal breakfast. >> Oh, yeah. Huge problem. >> So the source gives this really specific protocol to counter this. It's from uh Cara Collier and Forza Nasser. It's basically a two-step process if you're having a carb heavy meal. >> Step one, you drink a small amount of apple cider vinegar, obviously diluted in water before you eat. And then step two, 10 to 15 minutes of movement right after. >> But like let's unpack this. Why vinegar? >> It really comes down to the acetic acid. So the acetic acid in the apple cider vinegar actively slows down gastric emptying. >> Okay? >> It essentially acts as this um chemical break. It holds the food in your stomach longer so the carbs are released into your intestines and then into your bloodstream at a much slower manageable trickle instead of just a massive flood. And at the same time, because it's so bitter, it stimulates your liver to release bile, which optimizes your digestion right from the top down. >> Okay, so the ACV slows the incoming tie to sugar. Makes sense. Then we hit step two, the movement part. The sources talk about how skeletal muscles act as a massive glucose sink, and they soak up over 80% of circulating blood glucose when they're stimulated. >> Right? 80%. It's huge. >> But I mean, let's be totally realistic here for a second. If I just ate a huge heavy meal, my parasympathetic nervous system is kicking in. I just want to rest and digest. >> You want to take a nap. >> Exactly. The absolute last thing I want to do is put on gym clothes and go do a full workout. So, if my muscles are a sponge soaking up that glucose, do I need to like totally ring them out with a massive gym session or is a quick wipe down enough? >> Oh, it is definitely just a quick wipe down. In fact, an intense sweat inducing workout right after you eat would actually pull blood away from your digestive tract >> and give you terrible cramps probably. >> Yeah, exactly. You really just need enough contraction to trigger these things called GLUT4 receptors. >> Okay. What are those? >> So, normally these receptors need insulin to essentially open the cell doors and let the glucose in. But muscular contraction physically forces those glut4 receptors right to the surface of the cell membrane which completely bypasses the need for insulin. >> Wait, really? You're bypassing the insulin resistance entirely just by moving the muscle. >> Precisely. It's an entirely different biological pathway. The sources actually share this brilliant case study about Cara Collier herself. She's a dietitian living in Phoenix where, you know, summer temperatures hit like 120°. >> Yeah. Nobody is going for a brisk post lunch walk in Phoenix, >> right? No way. So instead, she opts for just brief sets of bodyweight squats and push-ups right there in her office just between meetings. And this micro movement, it's totally enough to force those glut receptors to the surface and pull the sugar right out of the bloodstream. >> And the so what for you listening is that flattening these spikes prevents the formation of advanced glycation end products or AGs. >> Yes, AGs are awful. >> I always visualize AGs like this um biological caramelization process, right? Like when you have too much sugar floating around, it binds to proteins and fats and just stiffens them. It literally caramelizes your tissue which accelerates cellular aging. So you are essentially preventing that caramelization just by doing a few office squats. >> Exactly. And that biological reality that muscle contraction forces glucose out of the blood, it directly dictates our next focus. Because if skeletal muscle is our primary sink for metabolic waste, we really have to talk about what happens when that sink starts to shrink as we age, >> right? And the protocols for maintaining that muscle mass from Dr. Ben Bickman, Dr. Eric Helms, and JJ Virgin, I mean, they are intense. >> They don't mess around. >> No, they don't. To halt muscle loss, especially if you're in a caloric deficit, they prescribe resistance training two to four times a week. But honestly, the nutrition data is what really caught my eye. >> The protein threshold. Yes, the sources point to this massive meta regression like 800 participants showing this linear dose response relationship for protein. They say the optimal threshold to completely halt muscle loss is 1.9 to 2.5 gram of protein per kilogram of fat-ree mass, >> which is a lot, >> right? I have to push back here. 2 and a half grams per kilo for an average guy. That is an enormous amount of food. I mean, how do you even consume that without getting massive gastrointestinal distress or, you know, the proverbial meat sweats? >> It's a very valid challenge for sure. And it's exactly why the application of the data matters just as much as the data itself. You absolutely cannot eat that in one or two sittings. >> Okay, good. Cuz I was feeling a little sick just thinking about it. >> Yeah, your body actually has a cap on how much muscle protein synthesis it can trigger at once. It usually maxes out around, say, 30 to 50 grams of high quality protein per meal. And that's specifically gated by an amino acid called leucine. So to hit that 1.9 to 2.5 gram threshold without completely destroying your digestion, you have to evenly distribute it across four or five meals throughout the day. >> Oh, I see. Because if you just sit down and eat a massive 100 g steak at dinner, most of that isn't actually building muscle. >> Exactly. It's just being oxidized for energy or well excreted. But the reason these experts are so aggressive about hitting that high threshold is because muscle is fundamentally misunderstood by most people. It isn't just, you know, structural weight to move your bones around. Muscle is actually a highly active endocrine organ, >> meaning it secretes hormones, >> right? When you engage in heavy resistance training, your muscles synthesize and release these signaling proteins called myioines. >> Okay. And how do those work? Because the source text actually refers to muscle as metabolic spanx since it pulls everything together, which is a funny visual, but it sounds like myocines are doing something way more complex than just, you know, holding your stomach in. >> Uh, way more complex. They act like this localized anti-inflammatory pharmacy inside your body. When muscles contract against heavy resistance, they release specific myioines like interlucan 6. And those travel through your bloodstream to your liver, your brain, your fat tissue, and they actively turn down systemic inflammation. >> Wow. >> But here's the catch, and this is the scary part. The fast twitch muscle fibers, which are the ones most responsible for releasing these protective mioines, they are the very first ones to atrophy when we become sedentary. >> Oh man. So, if you don't use them, you don't just get physically weaker. You literally lose your internal anti-inflammatory pharmacy. You lose the pharmacy, which leads to a terrifying downstream effect. The data shows that falling into a state of physical frailty increases your hospital mortality risk by a staggering 8.2 times. >> Two times. >> Yeah. A minor fall or this a basic infection becomes a lethal event because the body has zero metabolic reserve left to fight it. >> That is incredibly sobering. And I'm thinking if you're listening to this right now and thinking, "Well, I haven't lifted a weight in 10 years. My joints hurt and I already feel pretty fragile." You might be wondering if you've completely missed the window, >> like is it actually safe to start loading the joints with heavy resistance at that stage? >> It is not only safe, it is literally the only way out of the fragility trap. >> Really? >> Yeah. The text highlights this physical therapy patient who got a really severe osteoporosis diagnosis and she was just devastated. Her doctor essentially told her to stop all impact activities. She thought her active life was totally over, >> which is what a lot of people hear, >> right? But under proper supervision, she implemented just 30 minutes of resistance and balance training twice a week. >> So just 1 hour total per week. >> 1 hour a week. And the mechanical tension on her bones signaled them to increase density, and the muscle growth totally stabilized her joints. She quickly regained complete physical autonomy and was confidently navigating stairs without any fear of falling, >> which proves the body is never just static. It's always waiting for a signal to rebuild itself. And I think this perfectly sets up our next major insight. Because all this physical stamina we're building, the heavy lifting, the vascular health, it isn't just about the body. The primary beneficiary of all that physical output is actually sitting right inside your skull. >> Yes. This is where the biological connections become just incredible. The physical endurance you build in your muscles literally clears the path for profound structural changes in the brain. So, the sources outline this cardio structure called the Norwegian 4x4 protocol, which is heavily championed by Tim Ferrris and Dr. Maje Fatui. And honestly, it sounds brutal. >> It's not easy. >> It's four rounds of 4 minutes of high-intensity aerobic exercise separated by 3 minutes of active rest. You are deliberately pushing your cardiovascular system to its absolute maximum capacity. But mechanistically, why? We just talked about muscles releasing myioines, but how does gasping for air on a stationary bike actually grow the brain? >> It's all about how the body responds to acute stress. When you hit that high-intensity threshold in the 4x4 protocol, you are temporarily creating a hypoxic environment, meaning your brain and muscles are burning oxygen faster than your lungs can actually supply it. >> Okay. >> The brain registers this metabolic stress and responds by triggering mitochondrial biogenesis. >> It'll do more engines. >> Exactly. It physically builds more energy producing mitochondria inside your neurons so they can handle the stress better the next time and simultaneously it triggers a massive flood of brain derived neurotrophic factor or BDNF. >> Now I've heard BDNF described a lot as miracle grow for the brain but I'll be honest that always felt a bit vague to me like miracle grow just makes plants bigger and it sounds like BDNF is doing something much more I don't know architectural. Yeah, Miracle Grow is entirely too passive of a metaphor. Think of your brain's neural network like a massive complex high-speed rail system. BDNF isn't just the electricity running through the wires. It's the track laying machine. Oh, >> I like that. >> Right. It actively promotes neuroplasticity by laying down new synaptic connections and reinforcing the insulation on existing pathways so signals can travel way faster. >> The track laying machine. That makes so much sense. And the data from the sources shows just how much track is actually being laid. They tracked this 12week intensive lifestyle program that used these exact stamina protocols. 84% of participants experienced objective cognitive improvements. >> 84% is wild. >> But the structural changes were what blew my mind. The participants successfully grew the physical volume of their hippocampus by 3%. And functionally, a 3% growth in the hippocampus equates to a brain that is literally 3 years younger. And Tim Ferrris notes that the structural volume changes from the 4x4 protocol can maintain that age reversal effect for up to 5 years. >> And the clinical outcomes of that are completely life-changing. Just look at the case of Carol, this 71-year-old patient detailed in the sources. She arrived at a brain clinic in a wheelchair, totally convinced she was facing a rapid, inevitable cognitive decline. She had lost all her independence. That's heartbreaking. >> It is. But after 12 weeks of progressively building her physical stamina and forcing that BDNF release, the track laying machine did its job. She was out of the wheelchair. Her energy levels rebounded. She was so sharp she was actually actively looking to rejoin the workforce. >> From a wheelchair to job hunting in 3 months. >> That is incredible. But um we do have to address the biological cost of all this, right? Building new mitochondria and laying down new neural tracks generates an enormous amount of metabolic waste in the brain. You can't just run the engine at red line all day long. You have to clear the exhaust. >> Absolutely. Which brings us to the glimpmphatic system. See, while you are awake, your brain is actively preventing waste clearance. It's way too busy just keeping you alive and processing the world. >> Makes sense. It is only during deep slowwave sleep that the brain cells physically shrink which allows cerebrros spinal fluid to rush in and wash all that metabolic exhaust out. >> And the sources show exactly what happens when you short change that process. Sleep deprivation doesn't just leave you feeling tired or groggy. It biologically reprograms you to gain weight. Just a few nights of poor sleep increases your hunger hormone grein by 20% and suppresses your sati hormone leptin by 15%. >> Because your brain interprets the lack of sleep as a literal crisis. If you are awake at 2 a.m. historically speaking, it meant you were in danger or you were starving. So it alters your hormones to make you crave hyper palatable calorie dense foods just to survive the perceived famine. So, how do we guarantee we get into that deep slowwave sleep to actually clear the exhaust? According to Dr. Michael Bruce in the text, it all hinges on one physiological requirement. To initiate sleep, your heart rate absolutely must drop below 60 beats per minute. >> Yeah, under 60. >> But you can't just forcefully tell your heart to slow down. So, Dr. Bruce focuses on timing and breath. He recommends anchoring your wake time perfectly to your genetic chronotype. you know, whether you are biologically wired as a lion, a bear, a wolf, or a dolphin. >> And the precision of that wake time is so critical because of the melatonin phase response curve. When sunlight hits your eyes at the exact same time every morning, it starts a biological timer in your brain. Exactly 14 hours later, your brain will naturally begin to secrete melatonin. You're basically surfing a hormonal wave that you initiated at breakfast. >> Okay? But even with melatonin flowing, if you get into bed and your mind is just racing about, you know, some dumb email you send at work, your heart rate isn't dropping below 60. >> No, you're stressed, >> right? To force that drop, Dr. Bruce introduces the 478 breathing method. You inhale for 4 seconds, you hold for 7 seconds, and exhale for 8 seconds, repeating that for 20 cycles. But what is the actual mechanism here? Like why those specific numbers? >> It's all about the prolonged exhale. When your exhale is twice as long as your inhale, it heavily stimulates the vagus nerve. The vagus nerve then releases a neurotransmitter called acetylcholine directly onto the heart, which acts as a literal break. It physically slows the electrical impulses that control your heart rate, forcing the cardiovascular system into a parasympathetic rest and digest state. >> Okay, the biology of that makes perfect sense to me. But in practice, counting breaths can be a massive trap. Like if I'm lying in the dark hyperfocused on inhaling for four, holding for seven, exhaling for eight, and trying to track 20 whole cycles, I inevitably lose count. >> Oh, everyone does. >> And then I get frustrated that I messed up, which spikes my performance anxiety, which spikes my adrenaline, and suddenly my heart rate is at 80, and I'm wide awake staring at the ceiling. >> It's a very common paradox. Yeah. Engaging the analytical part of your brain account actively fights the exact relaxation response you're trying to induce. But Dr. Bruce recognized this flaw and he actually hacked his own protocol. Instead of doing any mental math at all, he lies in bed and makes light fists with both hands under the covers. For every complete breath cycle, he just extends one finger. Once he hits 10 fingers, he reverses the process, bringing them back into a fist one by one. >> Wow. So, you completely bypass the prefrontal cortex. There's no counting, just a physical anchor. >> Exactly. It occupies the mind just enough to block those stressful ruminations, but it requires zero mathematical effort. It rapidly lowers sleep latency, which is the time it takes to fall asleep, and ensures you drop quickly into the deep enm sleep required for that glimpmphatic wash. >> That is remarkably simple. I love that. Okay, let's look at the whole system we've built here today. You're flattening your glucose curves with acetic acid and muscle contraction. You are optimizing your anti-inflammatory mioines with 2.5 grams of protein and heavy resistance. You're laying track in the brain with the Norwegian 4x4. And you are washing the brain's exhaust every night with Vegas nerve stimulation. I mean, it is a perfect biological machine. >> It really is. >> But here is the ultimate twist from the sources. You can perfectly execute the biology, but if the software running the machine is corrupted, the hardware still degrades. >> Yes. which is exactly why we have to examine the mind because your psychology is inextricably linked to your physiology. You can't separate them. >> And the data proving this is just jaw-dropping. Yeah. >> There is this massive long-term Yale study detailed in the text that tracks people's mindsets over time. They found that individuals in their 30s who held positive views about aging live 7.5 years longer than those with negative views. >> 7.5 years. >> 7.5 extra years. That is a larger lifespan increase than quitting smoking or significantly lowering your cholesterol. >> Yeah. >> But I need to understand the bridge here. How does a subjective thought in your head translate into actual biological aging? >> The mechanism bridging the two is a neurological concept called predictive processing. So your brain is trapped inside a dark skull, right? It doesn't actually see or experience the outside world directly. It actively constructs your reality by filtering sensory inputs through your expectations and beliefs. >> If you hold a limiting belief, for example, that aging is just a painful inevitable decline, your brain adopts that as the literal blueprint. >> So, it's constantly looking for evidence to prove the blueprint. Right. >> Exactly. And when it finds it, say you get a sore knee or you forget a name, it perceives it as a massive threat, which triggers a chronic stress response. you are basically bathing your cells in low-grade cortisol all day which drastically increases your alosatic load which is the overall wear and tear on your body. >> But if you flip it >> right if you hold a liberating belief that growth and neuroplasticity are possible at any age your brain filters reality completely differently. It interprets that same sore knee not as a threat but just as a normal signal to rest. The cortisol doesn't spike. The alosatic load drops. >> So your thoughts are literally commanding your hormonal state. But how do we actually intercept those negative blueprints when modern life is just constantly throwing stressors at us? The text highlights this behavioral tool from Near Isle and Dr. Mark Brackett called the meta moment. The idea is that when a stressor hits, you take a deliberate pause to breathe and actively envision your best self before you react. I look at the meta moment like a mental circuit breaker. You know, when a massive surge of electricity or stress hits the house instead of letting it run through the wires and fry all the appliances, the breaker physically pops. It buys you a second to assess before resetting the whole system. >> That circuit breaker analogy maps perfectly to the neuratomy. Actually, when a stressor hits your amygdala, which is the reactive emotional center in the lyic system, it wants to take immediate control. It just wants to fight or run. >> Right. Pure instinct. >> Exactly. The meta moment, that deliberate practice pause physically shifts the metabolic activity away from the lyic system and pushes it into the prefrontal cortex, which is the logical purposeful center of the brain. You are forcibly moving the electrical current from the reactive hardware to the intentional hardware. >> And when you teach people to use that circuit breaker, the environment around them totally transforms. There's this story in the text about a school implementing this that I just loved. A student was acting out on the playground, being super unkind. The teacher marched over, clearly frustrated, totally ready to discipline him. And this kid audaciously looked right at his teacher and said, "I think you need to take a meta moment first." >> Which is amazing. He essentially told her the circuit breaker needed to pop, >> right? But the crazy thing is it worked. The teacher paused, shifted the activity to her prefrontal cortex, and was able to view the situation with actual empathy rather than reactive anger. The text notes that during the pandemic, schools with leaders who actively utilize tools like the meta moment saw a 40% drop in burnout and frustration rates. >> That's a massive reduction. >> It is. When you reduce that perceived suffering and block the chronic stress loops, you maintain the motivation required to sustain all the other habits we've discussed today. Because if you are constantly burning out your nervous system with negative predictive processing, you won't have the energy to do the 4x4 protocol or cook your protein. The software dictates the hardware. And honestly, that's really the most actionable takeaway from this entire deep dive. Knowledge is completely useless unless you actually test it in your own biology. You don't need to implement all of this tomorrow. Just pick one mechanism to experiment with today. Drink the vinegar before dinner. Extend a finger while breathing tonight in bed. shift the blueprint. >> I completely agree. Just start with one mechanism and I want to leave you with a final thought to chew on regarding that Yale study in predictive processing. We started this deep dive talking about how the body isn't just a static house. It is constantly tearing itself down and rebuilding based on the signals you give it. If your brain literally filters reality based on your expectations and simply expecting a positive outcome buys you 7.5 extra years of biological life, what does that mean for the stressful catastrophic stories you might be telling yourself right now about your future? Is it possible that dreading the inevitable decline is the exact signal telling your cells to stop maintaining the armor? Thank you so much for joining us on this deep dive. Keep questioning the blueprint. Keep building.

So they are molecules similar to hormones that our own body produces. There are several types of these molecules on the market. Two very popular ones here in Brazil are semaglutide, which has the trade name Ozenique, and tirepathide, which has the trade name Monjaro and has become quite popular, right? So, understand first that this is a medication, a synthetic molecule that will mimic the action of a hormone that our own body produces, right? It's important to reinforce, everyone, a brief historical aspect here: these medications were initially produced for the treatment of type 2 diabetes, which is the type of diabetes in which the individual is either unable to produce insulin adequately or the insulin is weak and unable to have the effect it should . So, are these medications still indicated for the treatment of this type of clinical condition? Because one of the effects of this medication, one of the effects of this hormone that our own body produces, GLP-1, is precisely to stimulate the production of insulin. So, for a person who has deficient production in some way, or whose hormone levels are relatively weak, medication can help by stimulating the production of that hormone, right? They were initially produced for that purpose, but eventually other effects and potential benefits began to be observed within some specific clinical contexts, more specifically the effect of aiding in the weight loss process, which is what made these medications really popular, right? Many people around the world, including here in Brazil, want to lose weight for numerous reasons. Some of these people are actually overweight or obese and need to lose weight for health reasons. But beyond that, another layer that I'm going to bring up for us to discuss is that there are other factors, other invisible forces, so to speak, which I'll talk about more at the end of the video, that may be encouraging people to seek weight loss, even if they don't need it. But regardless of anything else, folks, in fact, looking at the scientific literature—and I, along with my team, conducted a very extensive review of the scientific literature to understand where we stand regarding these medications, what their effects on the body and brain really are, their benefits and drawbacks. Looking at the literature, and being very honest with you, these medications do indeed have great potential to help with the weight loss process. So, when we look at the literature, we see that it can promote a weight loss of 10, 15, 20% of the original weight, right, over a period of approximately 70 weeks. I'm rounding off here because it largely depends on which medication is being used. It's the Ozempique, the Monjar, or some other [method], the dosage, the protocol, the person's lifestyle, whether they have habits of exercising or not. There are a number of factors that will ultimately affect weight loss, right? But just to give you an idea of the magnitude, we're talking about a significant weight loss here, right? From 10 to 20%, something around approximately 15 to 16 kg in a process over a period of 70 weeks, right? It really is a very powerful medication in helping with the weight loss process. But here I wanted to highlight an interesting point that I wanted to bring to our discussion, for the reflection of those of you who are considering its use, whether indicated or not. We're looking here at data from patients who were actually indicated to use it. These are usually patients who suffer from at least some type of obesity, or they have type 2 diabetes, as I mentioned a moment ago, right? And they, we're talking about data here obtained from a treatment period of approximately one and a half years, 70 weeks, right? So, this illustrates an interesting aspect of the use of these medications: they are usually part of a treatment plan, right? The medication, as I 'll explain in a little bit more detail, is never the treatment itself; it's part of a long- term treatment that can last many months, sometimes even years. But anyway, getting back to the point, folks, looking at the literature and being completely honest, it really is a medication that can be quite helpful in the weight loss process, right? So, what is the cause of this weight loss? It's very important that you understand this. What is actually causing a person to lose weight in this slimming process through the action of these medications, these slimming pens, right? These medications really do have a metabolic effect, right? As I mentioned to you, one of the effects of these molecules is to stimulate the production of insulin, which is one of the main hormones involved in our metabolism. It's not the only one, there are several others, but it's a very important one. It's that hormone that tends to be released after a meal. And it goes there, sorry experts, but it goes there, it acts by kind of taking the nutrients that have just entered and sending them inside the cells to be used as an energy source or to be stored in our energy reserves, right? So it's a very important hormone in our metabolism in general, and these medications will indeed have some metabolic effect, but the main factor that leads to weight loss is a behavioral change, right? These medications, as I will explain to you in detail, artificially promote a behavioral change. The individual begins to feel less appetite, less desire, less motivation, less willingness to eat, feels more satiated, and experiences what is characterized as a behavioral change in relation to food, which effectively leads to a lower calorie intake, resulting in weight loss. Now, everyone, let's make this clear, especially for those outside the field, that every change in behavior is the result of changes in brain activity, in specific areas of the brain, as I 'll explain to you in a moment. So, let's delve a little deeper into this so you understand, so it doesn't just remain a superficial thing, like, "oh, the medication reduces appetite." There is at least one potential harm that I don't see people discussing much, which I personally consider very relevant, and that is related to how these medications act on the brain, right? So let's take a step back to understand a little better and talk a little about basic physiology. What is the basic action of this hormone called GLP1, right? So, these medications, right, Zenpique again, ZPIC, Monjar, or any of that type, what they're doing is mimicking the action of a hormone, which is this hormone called GLP-1. Let's take a look at what GLP-1 is, when it's produced, how it's produced, and what effect it typically has on a person's body , right? GLP1, as I said, is an abbreviation for Glucagon-like Protein, a protein similar to Glucagon, okay? A protein that looks like another hormone called glucagon, but anyway, it's a hormone that's produced in the intestine, especially in the final segment of the intestine, in a portion that we call the... you don't need to memorize this, it's just for those who want to delve a little deeper, because I know there are many professionals who follow my content and maybe they are here studying, wanting to understand in more depth, right? And then I'll mention a few names, a few more things for those who want to delve deeper. But anyway, the important thing here is to understand that it's a hormone produced in the gut, and the main stimulus for its production is eating, it's feeding yourself, right? Even before eating, we can already experience some stimulation in the production of this hormone, especially in the brain. It 's not just the intestines that produce GLP-1, this hormone. We have areas in the brain that also produce this signal. So, even before you eat, you may already have GLP-1 being released in areas of the brain, already promoting some modulation, but the main stimulus for producing this hormone in sufficient quantity to be released into the bloodstream is the meal itself. So, when nutrients reach the intestine, especially the final portion of the intestine, we have specific cells there called L cells, which will release this hormone. This hormone will act in various places near the intestine itself, or in distant places like the brain, starting with what is closest, right? So, let's imagine here that it's a hormone, a chemical signal that is released in response to a meal, right? So, the chemical signal is telling the body in general that the meal is being eaten, that it has been eaten, that nutrients are arriving, and the body has to prepare to deal with that, right? This hormone acts in the stomach, which is one of the places where it acts. It acts in several other places; I 'll mention the main ones, right? It acts in the stomach, decreasing stomach motility. So, the chemical signal arrives in the stomach and says: "Stomach, hey, uh, nutrients are arriving in the intestine, there are already plenty of nutrients, so calm down, stop moving so much," because it's precisely the motility, the movement of the stomach, that causes the food to be digested a little inside the stomach and then propelled into the intestine. So, from the moment a chemical signal decreases the motility of the stomach, the food tends to stay longer in the stomach. It's as if it delays the digestion and absorption process of the food. It's a signal that's kind of regulating the process there, right? So, if nutrients are arriving in the final portion of the intestine, it's because there are already plenty of nutrients in the intestine that are processing and absorbing food, and it tries to hold things back a little in the stomach. As I also mentioned, another place where GLP-1 will act, and I'm talking about normal people, okay? In the body of anyone who isn't even doing [something unclear - possibly a medical procedure]. Using one of these medications or not , right? It's a hormone that the body itself produces. So, once a meal is eaten, this hormone is released, and it also acts on the pancreas, stimulating insulin production. Makes sense, right? So, nutrients are being absorbed, stimulating a hormone that will participate in the uptake of these nutrients and cause them to enter the body's cells in general so that this nutrient can be used or stored as a reserve, right? So, it decreases stomach motility, stimulates the production and release of insulin, right? And that's precisely one of the reasons why these medications are also used in the treatment of type 2 diabetes, okay? Now, perhaps more interesting for our discussion here are the effects that these hormones have on the brain and on a person's behavior , on the brain, on sensation, motivation, perception, and behavior in general. And this hormone can act on various areas of the brain through two main pathways that I will only mention again here for those who want to delve a little deeper, okay? So this hormone... It's produced in the gut, right? And it can act locally on a set of nerves, let's say , that connect the gut to the brain. There are several ways in which the brain and gut communicate, what we call the gut-brain axis. There are several ways. The brain and gut are communicating a lot; one influences the other. I'll even give a very interesting example here of how the gut can affect brain function and consequently a person's mind and behavior. So, this GLP-1 hormone, which is produced during or after a meal, can act on a set of nerves that connect the gut to the brain. One nerve, in fact, specifically a very famous and important one, called the vagus nerve, right? For those who aren't in the field, imagine it 's a wire connecting the brain to the gut. And then the GLP-1 hormone goes to the tip of this wire connected to the gut, signals there, and this generates a signal that reaches the brain and modulates the release of GLP-1 in the brain itself, right? So this is one way... This GLP-1 hormone, produced in the intestine, can affect the functioning of areas of the brain. Another, more obvious way is that the hormone is released into the bloodstream, circulates throughout the body, and eventually reaches the brain. It has to cross a barrier that separates the blood from the brain, which we call the blood-brain barrier. It's like a barrier, like the barriers we have around countries that allow some people to pass through while others don't. We have this in the brain. Not just anything can enter the brain. GLP-1 is a molecule that can enter the brain; it's a hormone that can enter and access areas of the brain, at least in some places, although we don't yet know exactly what the mechanisms behind this are. But anyway, another way the hormone acts on the brain is by entering the bloodstream, reaching the brain, and acting on various areas . And here's a very important point: when we look at the areas of the brain that have a receptor, a place for this hormone to bind and produce some effect... In effect, the number of areas is surprisingly large, right? And there are many areas involved in generating the desire for food, generating the urge to eat, generating the urgency to eat, generating the perception of hunger. All of this, all these sensory, perceptual, and behavioral elements related to the act of eating are coordinated by specific areas that are modulated by this hormone. And I would like to mention here for those who want to delve deeper, which areas I am referring to. So, one of these areas is the famous hypothalamus, which is a region generally involved in keeping us alive. It's there monitoring body temperature, controlling our metabolism, and controlling, to some degree, our eating behavior, right? It's a region that signals that it's time to eat. It's been a while since you had a meal, it's time to eat, it participates in generating that experience that we call hunger, right? Another set of areas that is also modulated by this hormone, GLP1, are those areas that together we call the reward system, which you probably already know. I've heard a lot about it, that it's associated with habit formation, addiction, motivation, desire, and the pursuit of rewards in general. So, we have several areas there, just to mention a few: the ventral tegmental area, which is one of the main places of dopamine production; the nucleus accumbens, an important region in making us seek things, having motivation to do things, right? Uh, uh, the orbitofrontal cortex, an area of the brain involved in evaluating the value of possible behaviors, including eating habits, right? So this set of areas that together we call the reward system, they have a place where this hormone can bind and modulate the activity of these areas. Two other interesting areas that are modulated by this hormone are the amygdala, a region essentially involved with emotional processing, so those emotions associated with eating—and there's a lot of eating, many people eat because of emotional issues. So, perhaps that feeling of urgency to eat something specific at the moment a person is feeling bad, this will probably involve the activity of this area that we call the amygdala, which will activate there, generating That feeling of urgency, right? And another region called the insula, which is an important region in building our internal perception of our body. So, that perception of hunger or even disgust, is an area that will participate in this perception in a general way. So, what is the result of this modulation by this GLP-1 hormone in these areas of the brain? The person tends to feel more satiated in general, right? So, the meal tends to be more satiating, let's say. So, a smaller meal will already satiate the person. The period between meals, in which the person feels satiated, tends to be longer. The person tends to feel more satiated. Their appetite decreases, the desire for food decreases. When they see A food that might previously have been a trigger, making her crave Nutella, a hamburger, or a pizza, she doesn't feel as much desire for, doesn't feel as much appetite for, doesn't feel as much motivation to seek out food, to actually eat. So, in fact, and it makes sense, right? Because think about it, we're talking about a hormone produced in the gut that's associated with meals. Its function is precisely to signal to the body: "The meal is over, you can stop eating, you don't need to eat anymore." It tells the stomach: "Stomach, stop moving because there are already plenty of nutrients in the gut." It tells the pancreas: "Pancreas, produce insulin because nutrients are coming in. We need to deal with it and metabolize it correctly." And it signals various areas of the brain: "Brain, there's food here, you don't need to eat anymore, you do n't need to seek out more, you don't need to expend energy on this." And that's effectively what happens, right? Now, an important point is that I'm talking about the normal action of this hormone. And the normal action of this hormone is that the body... Everyone produces this hormone, and it's temporary, right? So, when you're about to eat a meal, the tendency is for the levels of this hormone to increase a little before, during, and after you have a peak, right? But then the levels of these hormones drop to basal levels, right? It has its function, but after the levels drop, it stops acting, right? So, this whole effect we're talking about is usually periodic, okay? And having said all that, folks, I hope it's clear, and I apologize if I'm bringing up too many names. I want more, it's more for those who really want to delve deeper into this subject, right? But having said that, I hope the action of this hormone in our body is clear. If I ask you how medications act in the body, you'll know the answer, right? So, when we look at studies to see the effect of these medications, what we see is very curious. So, in a normal individual, especially if they are hungry, when you show them a picture of food they like, right? When you see food you like, the tendency is to activate, in a way, the activity of these areas. You see an increase in the activity of these areas involved with desire, hunger, motivation, and eating habits , which is precisely what's behind making a person think, "I want that, I'm going to get that, I'd like to eat that, that would be good," without desire, right? When a person is using this medication, these areas have reduced activity, right? Which explains the lower desire the person will have, the lower will they will have to eat. And it's curious, something the data shows is that this inhibition of the activity of these areas in the context of eating seems to be more evident when we show high-calorie foods. So what we're seeing is a reduction in activity under the effects of medications, right, LP1 agonists, Monjarosque and everything else. We see an inhibition of the activity of the Areas involved with desire, motivation, and habits are activated when a person sees an image of food, especially a high-calorie food, which is precisely the type that should activate these areas even more . So, what we're seeing here is that these medications are modulating these areas in a way that makes the person want less, seek less, desire less, right? And I'm going to open an interesting parenthesis here, folks, to comment with you on the following: in some cases of obesity, these areas involved with desire, motivation, and habit actually tend to respond even more to the image of food than normal, right? This is a functional alteration that we can observe in the brains of at least some people who suffer from obesity. When we look, it seems that the person's brain is hyper-reactive to food cues. So, when you show a food cue, in a normal person, you will see an increase in the activity of these areas involved with hunger, desire, motivation, and everything else. This medication comes in and lowers the volume, like, " don't react so much," it modulates the areas there, right? The medication itself goes there and modulates the activity of these areas, causing them not to respond as much. And in the case of these obese individuals, what is curious is that at the same time that these circuits are hyperreactive to food cues, they seem to activate less after the individual has ingested food. So those areas involved in generating the sensation of reward and sometimes even pleasure tend to be less active than normal. So it's reversed. Before eating they are hyperactive, making the person crave more than normal. And after the meal, some of these areas are hypoactive, they activate less. This leads to a relatively lower feeling of satiety. It's a pattern we observe even in cases of addiction, right? Some specific drug, where this is quite characteristic. The urge to use the drug is disproportionately high, and the satisfaction after use is relatively lower, right? Which generates a vicious cycle, right? So, in some cases of obesity, we can observe this type of neurological and behavioral alteration in the context of eating. And medication is very useful in this scenario because it modulates precisely these areas, making the individual desire less, have less motivation, and seek less, while at the same time feeling relatively more satiated. So, going back to the point, understand that this medication, like any other medication, has associated risks, possible side effects, and potential harms that you need to understand, especially those of you who are considering using the medication or are already using it. Regardless of whether you have a prescription for the medication or not, it's important to know the potential harms associated with the medication. The first one I wanted to draw attention to is, actually, taking a step back, right? An understanding that I wanted you to have. As I mentioned earlier, this hormone GL LP1 is produced by our own body, right? The difference is that our own body produces it within a specific rhythm associated with the meal. When we eat, the hormone levels decrease, then fall, increase at the next meal, then fall again. So what we see is this hormone fluctuating throughout the day in association with meals. When a person uses this type of medication, this molecule will be acting all the time, or almost all the time, right? The treatment is designed for that purpose, so the molecule acts there as if it were an artificial brake that's always on , right? So we have to imagine that under the effect of the medication, these areas, including the stomach and pancreas, will be modulated and influenced at all times. This comes at a price, it definitely comes at a price. It's the equivalent of putting a turbocharger on a car. The car will go faster. It will go faster, but there's a price to pay. You'll wear out the engine more, you 'll use up the oil more, the engine's lifespan will be reduced, and other things like the brakes not being as good in corners. They're good on the straightaways, but not so good in corners. Ultimately, we always pay a price, and it's no different with this medication, right? And the first possible price that I would like you to understand you will pay is that, perhaps once you start using this medication and effectively begin to consume fewer calories and effectively lose weight, understand that depending on how you are doing it, especially if you are doing it alone, without the supervision of a qualified professional, you may end up creating a kind of dependence on this medication to maintain your appetite, your desire. reduced size and relatively low weight. So, I understand that these medications work like a kind of artificial chemical brake that's circulating in your body and acting in various places. It's decreasing your appetite, your desire, your motivation. You're craving less, eating less, and everything else. But from the moment you stop taking that medication, you'll be removing that brake. Appetite, desire, and motivation will return, perhaps even with a rebound effect. Perhaps. That's not what the scientific literature shows, but it's a possibility that once you stop the medication, interrupt the treatment with the medication, your appetite will return. You 'll feel the urge again, you'll feel desire again. If you haven't developed greater self-control or promoted more lasting changes in your habits over this period , you're likely to go back to square one. And in fact, when we look at the literature, what we see is that interrupting treatment is associated with significant weight regain. At home, he regains between 30% and 70% of the weight he lost. Sometimes the individual needs to resume using the medication. After undergoing treatment for a long time, they stop, a long time passes, they gain the weight back, and they have to start treatment again. So , folks, what's new is that, ultimately, we're talking about behavioral change in this case, dietary change. So, the person uses the medication, great, you'll feel less desire, you 'll eat less, but if you don't make other changes that will be more lasting, once you stop the medication, everything will go back to square one. Your weight will return to zero. Perhaps you feel even worse because now you have to compare it to when you were thinner. Ultimately, you could end up creating a dependency and causing even greater suffering in the future if you simply use this medication without planning, okay? So there's a possibility that you could develop a kind of dependence on the medication to keep your desires down and your weight at a lower level, right? Second category of potential harms associated with these medications. As you may already know, there is a relatively high probability that once you start using these medications, you will experience some type of gastrointestinal symptom. So think about it, it's a hormone that is released by your intestines. When you defecate, it affects various parts of the body, including the gastrointestinal tissue, as I mentioned earlier. One of its effects is to decrease stomach motility, which means that food will stay there longer, right? So it's very common to observe gastrointestinal symptoms, such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, reflux, constipation, and a feeling of fullness, precisely because you're interfering with a system that impacts, among other things, the gastrointestinal tissue. According to an estimate I was able to gather from the scientific literature, approximately 60 to 75% of people who use the medication experience some type of gastrointestinal symptom. It's actually very common, you must have heard of people using it. And then it says: "But that 's not a big deal. It can be a big deal, it's quite uncomfortable, right? Gastrointestinal symptoms, right? Nausea, vomiting are very uncomfortable, they have a relatively large potential to decrease your quality of life, right? And it can, and of course it varies a lot from person to person, but it can reach a point where the person interrupts the treatment. Even people who have an indication to use this medication, eventually they interrupt it. However magical and promising the medication may be, however tempting the medication may be, especially for those seeking weight loss, sometimes the gastrointestinal discomfort is so great that it makes the person abandon the treatment. Just so you have an idea of how uncomfortable this can be, especially at the beginning of treatment, right? So, there is a great deal of discomfort in having to deal with gastrointestinal symptoms. This is a major, a second major possible harm associated with the use of the medication. And third, but no less important, is that there is some literature indicating that the use of this medication is associated with a greater loss of lean mass." The individual will lose weight. Yes. Part of it is fat mass and part is lean mass. What do I mean by that? Part of the weight, the majority, generally speaking, is fat, and a portion, around 20 to 30%, is muscle, right? With the use of medication, if we compare people who are losing weight naturally, let's say , without any medication assistance, and people who are losing weight with the use of medication, we tend to see a greater loss of muscle in people who are using these medications, right? Especially if the person is not having adequate nutritional monitoring, and if they have a lower protein intake. Why? Appetite decreases, the desire for food decreases, the person ends up ingesting less food, fewer calories, including protein. And this can contribute to the person losing more lean mass. And it's a type of tissue that is relatively difficult to recover, right? Because in addition to diet, you need specific stimuli, such as strength training, weightlifting, to develop the tissue. - High vs Low Reps (Science-Based): Kind: captions Language: en High Reps or low reps which is better many people believe that aiming for a high rep count is better for cutting and getting very defined muscles with visible striations meanwhile low reps are better at building more muscle mass and bulking up however neither of these statements is actually true and there are many outdated misconceptions that people still have when it comes to choosing the best rep range for their goals so in this video we'll go over the benefits and drawbacks of each so that you can choose the best rep range for yourself let's first Define exactly what I mean when I say high or low reps High Reps when it comes to weight training typically involve sets that are greater than 12 reps per set 12 to 20 reps is the standard range as doing more than 20 reps will often lead to a breakdown in form due to the amount of fatigue that sets in meanwhile low reps are commonly associated with any rep range from 1 to six reps per set but usually you'll stick within a range of 3 to six reps to minimize the chance of injury obviously low reps are usually performed in combination with a much heavier weight load meanwhile High Reps requ require a lighter weight load the idea that high repetitions inherently lead to more defined muscles is a very outdated and common misconception having more cut visible muscles is usually referred to as muscle definition or muscle tone and both are primarily influenced by two factors muscle size and body fat percentage while high repetition training can contribute to muscle size it doesn't have a direct correlation with building more defined muscles you're not going to burn significantly more fat from doing High Reps either and there's no way to Target fat burn to specifically burn the fat away from the muscles you're working it might feel like that's what's happening from the enhanced muscle burning sensation and the bigger pump that you'll experience during High rep workouts but even though High Reps can increase blood flow to the muscles you're working and they can create a temporary feeling of tightness and fullness this does not translate to long-term muscle definition so when it comes to muscle definition aside from genetics the two biggest factors that are actually in your control are building your muscle side through resistance training while also reducing overall body fat percentage which mostly boils down to maintaining a proper diet plan without addressing the body fat component even well-developed muscles will stay hidden underneath a layer of body fat so regardless of your rep range you can still get very defined muscles just by building them up and staying lean that's right both High rep training and low rep training can be very effective for muscle growth in their own ways for example let's start with high rep training metabolic stress is a big component of muscle growth and it's generally higher when performing High Reps rather than low reps this metabolic stress triggers the release of growth hormone and igf-1 which both play a key role in boosting muscle protein synthesis and helping with the muscle repair and growth process typically during High rep sets the muscles that you're working will also get a greater amount of tuut or time under tension not only will you feel more fatigued the longer a muscle is activated during a set but more time under tension also stimulates the recruitment of both slow twitch and fast twitch muscle fibers so even though fast twitch fibers are in fact more associated with heavier weight loads while slow twitch fibers are more associated with higher reps better resistance to fatigue and improvements in endurance-based activities the constant stress placed on your muscles during High rep training will actually engage both fiber types effectively the major downfall of high rep training in terms of muscle growth is when you go overboard with reps if you try to do any exercise for 50 or 100 reps like let's say weighted squats for example there's a high chance that your form will start to break down or you'll need to take a break due to excessive fatigue and exhaustion rather than your muscles actually failing this is why I suggest if you're trying to build muscle with high rep training still use heavy enough weights that limit you to under 20 reps before you fail or at least come close to failure now low rep training offers its own unique set of advantages for muscle growth for example since it's associated with heavier weight loads it requires the recruitment of a higher percentage of muscle fibers specifically the fast twitch fibers which have a greater potential for overall growth low rep training also places a very high demand on your central nervous system leading to beneficial neural adaptations like increased muscle motor unit Recruitment and improved coordination between your nervous system and your muscles this is how low rep training can lead to much greater strength gains than high rep training increased strength can then allow you to lift heavier weights which contributes to Progressive overload consistently increasing the overall stress placed on your muscles over time is one of the main factors that leads to Greater overall muscle growth and low rep training helps you do just that the downfall with low rep training is twofold first of all low rep training requires heavy weight loads to be effective and those heavy weight loads Place more strain on your joints and connective tissues if you have improper form with heavier weight loads it can easily lead to injuries like muscle strains joint inflammation or full out tears the other issue is that low rep training doesn't stimulate slow twitch muscle fibers very effec effectively so muscle fatigue can actually lead to a strength Plateau this is why purely for muscle building purposes I recommend combining both high and low reps for example a very simple way to structure this is to perform 3 to six Reps for 3 weeks and then switch to 12 to 20 Reps for 3 weeks each of these rep ranges should be done with a heavy enough weight load that takes you to failure within that rep range by switching back and forth like this you get the best of both worlds this is because it's important to realize that muscle strength and muscle endurance can each independently cause a plateau so by working on both you can actually get stronger Target slow and fast twitch muscle fibers more effectively and build muscle faster so when your muscles are put through the demands of the high rep periods your body will actually respond by increasing the number and the efficiency of mitochondria within your cells and becoming more resistant to fatigue mitochondria are referred to as the PowerHouse of the cell because they play a crucial role in energy production this this improved ability to use oxygen for sustained energy will carry over to your lower rep periods then those lower rep periods will place a much higher mechanical load on the muscles which will increase the size and strength of the contractile proteins within the muscle fibers like actin and myosin this increase in force generation will then carry over to your high rep periods ultimately you're creating a cyclical upward spiral this is why you're likely to notice that each time that you switch rep ranges you'll be able to perform a heavier weight load than the last time you were at that same rep range the improvements from one rep range transfer over to the other helping you break through plateaus and that's the whole point now of course if your goal is purely performance rather than muscle growth you may want to choose one rep range over the other and stick to it that's because activities such as long-distance running cycling rowing and longdistance swimming are all much more dependent on muscular endurance and those slow twitch muscle fibers so if your goal was to perform your best at a marathon it would make sense to focus Fus Less on squatting the heaviest weight load you could force out for three reps and instead focus more on higher reps to develop your endurance capacity working specifically on these higher rep ranges will make your body adapt so that it can more efficiently utilize oxygen and clear out metabolic by products it'll also increase your ratio of slow twitch fibers which like I said are highly efficient at utilizing oxygen to generate energy on the other hand if your goal is to get better at sprinting powerlifting or explosive sports with short bursts of activity like football then you may be better off focusing on Lower rep ranges these lower rep ranges will improve power output explosiveness and central nervous system efficiency this can help you tackle harder Sprint faster or lift significantly heavier weight loads there are also other sports that require both fast twitch and slow twitch fiber such as soccer boxing and wrestling these activities have short intense bursts of explosive movement coupled with prolonged periods of sustained muscle activation that requires endurance for example a boxer needs to bounce around the ring sometimes for 12 full rounds while also throwing bursts of explosive punches in between so it's beneficial for these kind of activities to perform both High Reps and Low Reps during your workout routine and remember the 3 weeks of low reps followed by 3 weeks of High Reps is just one way to set up your routine to get the benefits of both you can also do both within one workout for example let's pretend you're trying to increase your push strength as well as your endurance you always want to perform your heaviest compound lifts first so that fatigue isn't as much of a limiting factor in the amount of weight you can lift so you can start your workout with three really heavy sets of bench press for three to six reps then for your next three sets you could perform six to eight reps of dumbbell presses with a drop set of 20 reps at the end of each set so you would use a heavy weight for 6 to8 reps then immediately drop to a lighter weight load for 20 reps alternatively instead of the 20 reps of dumbbell presses you can perform push-ups to failure at the end of each heavy heavy dumbbell press set this would allow you to train muscle strength and muscle endurance all within one session and there are actually many other ways that you can set this up in a nutshell When comparing one rep range against the other there is no one best rep range it highly depends on your goals but to put it simply there are individual benefits to each rep range research and organizations like the National Academy of sports medicine have shown that a rep range of 1 to six per set is best for increasing muscle strength than 7 to 12 reps per set is most often recommended for increasing muscle growth and 12 or more reps per set are best for increasing muscle endurance however by performing all of these rep ranges you can get benefits from each of them and that can be invaluable especially when it comes to breaking through plateaus and sticking points so that about wraps it up I really hope you've enjoyed this video if you have make sure you subscribe to my channel and if you want a done for you program that lets you stop wasting time and helps you skip all the that trial and error you can register now for my free 6 we sh you'll go through a streamlined process that includes full workout programs with a guided video exercise Library a personalized meal plan a 42 recipe cookbook and a coach to guide you through the entire program to find out more head on over to my website where like I said you can get all of this for free as long as you put your best foot forward and simply stick to the plan we create for you for the 6 weeks by sticking to this plan you'll get incredible results and we'll get an incredible before and after picture to find out more about our free six we sh click the link below in the description or you can head straight on over to the website at gravity transformation.com I'll see you guys [Music] soon

There's this valve at the bottom of the esophagus called the lower esophageal sphincter that's meant to open up. So when food comes in, it can come on in. And then when we start to acidify that food so that we can break the food down and get the nutrients out of that food once it starts to acidify, that valve closes so the acid doesn't come back up and burn us. But the problem is this valve is triggered by stomach acid. So if someone only has a small amount of stomach acid but not enough to trigger this valve, then the small amount of acid they have is going to come back up and burn them. So in most cases of acid reflux, we're seeing a lack of stomach acid, not too much stomach acid. And we should really dig into this more. So when we're looking at these studies and we're looking at chot and cholestasis, what that means, cholestasis just means that this bile that's in the gallbladder is not flowing down and helping us digest our food the way that it should. So the liver makes this bile, and then it comes down here and it stores it in the gallbladder for when it's ready to be used. And that gallbladder's job is to store it and then concentrate that bile so that a small amount of bile can be more effective. So once food is acidified in the stomach and leaves the stomach and comes down here into the duodenum, that triggers this gallbladder to squirt this alkaline bile down. And the bile is meant to help us emulsify or break down our dietary fats so we can access fat soluble vitamins like A, E, D, and K. And it's also meant to help us neutralize the acids that are leaving the stomach because it's more alkaline than these acids. And then all these activities also trigger the pancreas to squirt out things like enzymes and bicarb that is also alkaline, helps us neutralize these acids. So all of these functions are very important for our ability to break our food down so that we can break it down into the elemental nutrients that the body needs to function correctly. So when this gallbladder becomes too thick and sticky to flow correctly, which is very common, then it doesn't come down here and help us digest our food correctly. It doesn't trigger all these processes that help neutralize the acids that are leaving the stomach. So when that's the case, now, these acids continue to move through here where they don't belong. And remember we talked about how the body's going to say, well, we can't have that. So during the day, the body should be in this catabolic state where it's very good at creating energy, kind of keeping us going all day, but it's also good at breaking things down so that they can be rebuilt and renewed. And then at night, we're supposed to move into this anabolic state where the body's very good at sleeping and resting and repairing and rebuilding. So both of these states are appropriate. The problem is some people can get really stuck in one of these states most of the time, or maybe they're just crammed way too far into one of these states. And when they're really stuck in one of these states, it can create a wide variety of health issues. And one of the things that we want to understand is that when someone's stuck in this catabolic state, the body will send more of the water to the bowels and less to the kidneys. So with more water going to the bowels, that can create a chronic diarrhea issue. If someone's really stuck in this anabolic state, then it sends more water to the kidneys and less to the bowels. A lot of these folks will be up peeing 8, 9, 10 times a night because the body's sending so much of the water to the kidneys. place that could be the reason why you're not getting the results so these numbers for calorie deficits that you might find from an online calculator are only very rough estimates of what your body you need to be in a deficit or what your maintenance calories are tracking Cal calories is notoriously difficult to do even dietitians have been shown to be off by about 20 percent so don't feel bad adaptation that happens something called adaptive thermogenesis so any form of calorie restriction whether you're doing it through calorie counting or keto or intermittent fasting will cause your total daily energy expenditure to fall this is your body's protective mechanism against starvation usually it's around a 10 or a 15 percent decrease so your body's actually slowing down the rate of calorie burn independent of what size you are so that's something that you need to overcome so let's say you've been dieting for three or four months and you've been getting that calorie deficit So,&nbsp;&nbsp; if you're wondering what is protein and why it&nbsp; matters. So, first, what is protein and why does&nbsp;&nbsp; everyone especially people on GLP1 medications&nbsp; keep hearing about it? Protein is one of the three&nbsp;&nbsp; major macronutrients along with carbohydrates and&nbsp; fats. Think of protein as your body's construction&nbsp;&nbsp; crew. Protein builds and repairs muscle,&nbsp; hair, skin, hormones, and enzymes. Basically,&nbsp;&nbsp; everything that makes you, you. When you're losing&nbsp; weight, especially quickly on GLP-1 medications,&nbsp;&nbsp; your body doesn't just lose fat. It loses muscle,&nbsp; too. And protein is your main defense against&nbsp;&nbsp; muscle loss. So, skipping protein is like firing&nbsp; your body's repair team halfway through a major&nbsp;&nbsp; renovation and then wondering why the building is&nbsp; falling apart. Why GLP1 medications make protein&nbsp;&nbsp; more important? GLP-1/GIP medications like ozempic&nbsp; and mounjaro mimic natural hormone that slows your&nbsp;&nbsp; digestion and reduces appetite. That's great for&nbsp; weight loss, but here's the catch. People often&nbsp;&nbsp; eat way less than before because these medications&nbsp; are so effective at suppressing appetite. And this&nbsp;&nbsp; means you likely will end up consuming way less&nbsp; protein than what your body needs. A 2021 New&nbsp;&nbsp; England Journal of Medicine study showed that&nbsp; participants on semaglutide lost up to 40% of&nbsp;&nbsp; their total weight from lean mass, meaning muscle,&nbsp; not just fat. That's not because the medication is&nbsp;&nbsp; bad. It's because the body wasn't getting enough&nbsp; building material to protect muscle. And less&nbsp;&nbsp; muscle means slower metabolism, lower strength,&nbsp; fatigue, and more weight regained later. It's the&nbsp;&nbsp; opposite of what we want. Let's understand goal&nbsp; weight versus ideal weight as this is something&nbsp;&nbsp; we use for daily protein recommendations. Now,&nbsp; people often ask, should I calculate protein based&nbsp;&nbsp; on my current weight, my goal weight, or my ideal&nbsp; weight? Good question. Here's how I explain it to&nbsp;&nbsp; my patients. Your current weight is your starting&nbsp; point. Your goal weight is what you're working&nbsp;&nbsp; towards, but your ideal weight is what your body&nbsp; would weigh if you were at a healthy composition,&nbsp;&nbsp; not underweight, not overweight. For example,&nbsp; let's say if someone has current weight of 200 lb,&nbsp;&nbsp; height is 5'6 in sex, let's assume female. So&nbsp; to calculate ideal body weight common medical&nbsp;&nbsp; formula used to calculate ideal body weight&nbsp; is divine formula. Women 100 lb plus 5 lb for&nbsp;&nbsp; every inch over 5 ft. For men 106 lb plus 6 lb for&nbsp; every inch over 5t. So for a woman who is 5' 6 in&nbsp;&nbsp; tall 100 + 6 into 530 lb. For 5'6 tall man 106&nbsp; + 6 into 6 142 lbs. So ideal body weight for a&nbsp;&nbsp; woman who is 5'6 in tall would be 130 lb while for&nbsp; a man it would be 142 lb. There are other formulas&nbsp;&nbsp; to calculate ideal body weight like Robinson&nbsp; and Miller formula. You can pause and look at&nbsp;&nbsp; this slide if you interested. Are you ready for&nbsp; more numbers? Let's take a look at protein intake&nbsp;&nbsp; goals based on clinical evidence for 130 lbs.&nbsp; Ideal body weight. Remember, we use ideal body&nbsp;&nbsp; weight. Let's say ideal body weight of 130 lbs in&nbsp; a female. To prevent protein deficiency, you need&nbsp;&nbsp; 36 grams of protein per ideal body weight. Around&nbsp; 45 to 50 grams of protein per day. Remember,&nbsp;&nbsp; this is the number to prevent deficiency, not&nbsp; for optimal health. For sedentary lifestyle&nbsp;&nbsp; like minimal activity 045 to 0.55 grams per pound&nbsp; of ideal body weight. So around 60 to 70 grams of&nbsp;&nbsp; protein per day. For general exercise or light&nbsp; activity 0.55 to 73 grams per pound of ideal&nbsp;&nbsp; body weight. So 70 to 95 grams of protein per day.&nbsp; For weight loss or calorie deficit 73 to 1 g per&nbsp;&nbsp; pound of ideal body weight. So around 95 to 130&nbsp; grams of protein per day. for GLP1 medication use&nbsp;&nbsp; with resistance training. Now, this is the one you&nbsp; should be interested in the most would be 0.8 to&nbsp;&nbsp; 1.1 g per pound of ideal body weight. So, 105 to&nbsp; 145 g of protein per day. Remember, these are all&nbsp;&nbsp; recommendations for 130 lbs ideal body weight in&nbsp; a female. Your current body weight may be 150 or&nbsp;&nbsp; 170 or 200 lb. However, you need to start by&nbsp; calculating your ideal body weight. For men,&nbsp;&nbsp; protein intake recommendations will be slightly&nbsp; higher as their ideal body weight happens to be&nbsp;&nbsp; slightly higher than women. So&nbsp; key takeaways for a 5' 6 in tall 200lb female&nbsp;&nbsp; on GLP-1 medication. My recommendation&nbsp;&nbsp; is to aim for a higher number on this target.&nbsp; So around 130 to 140 grams protein per day.&nbsp;&nbsp; This recommendation aligns with clinical guidance&nbsp; from the international journal of obesity protes nutrition guidelines in 2020 for optimizing muscle&nbsp; retention during weight loss. So this was a lot of&nbsp;&nbsp; numbers and calculations. And then we have the aerobic system, which is like your endurance work.
